issue_title: dumping should fail for unsupported data types
issue_body:
At present, if an object of unsupported data type (e.g. tuple) is provided to `_dump_value`, it is simply returned as-is and is written to the toml string or file in its string representation (but without quotes). This gives the user the impression that there is nothing wrong with the supplied data. However, not only is the created toml string/file invalid, but toml.load[s] cannot read in the object. 

Propose raising error in `_dump_value` if `type(v) not in dump_funcs`.

Example current (bad) behavior:
```
>>> config_start = {'the_tuple': (3, 4, 5)}
>>> toml_conf = toml.dumps(config_start)
>>> config_end = toml.loads(toml_conf)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/shared/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/toml.py", line 373, in loads
    multibackslash)
  File "/shared/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/toml.py", line 478, in _load_line
    value, vtype = _load_value(pair[1], _dict, strictly_valid)
  File "/shared/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/toml.py", line 664, in _load_value
    v = int(v)
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '(3, 4, 5)'
```

Expected behavior:
```
>>> config_start = {'the_tuple': (3, 4, 5)}
>>> toml_conf = toml.dumps(config_start)
Traceback blah...
    blah...
    blah...
TypeError: `tuple` type not supported in TOML.
```

issue_title: Client gets stuck in disconnected state
issue_body:
Lately with [pmxbot](/yougov/pmxbot), we're observing a condition where intermittently (about once a week) the client, a SingleServerIRCBot, will lose its connection to the server, and once in that state, any attempts to transmit messages results in a ServerNotConnectedError exception.

The server should probably respond more robustly under this condition, either automatically reconnecting or by documenting a recommendation for how a client implementation should handle this condition.

issue_body:
I'm trying to code a program which prints the total number of packets based on same sending source port and same recieving destination port I want the output like:

Src Dst Srcport Destport Packets

10.1.1.1 10.1.1.2 50 5052 50

10.1.1.1 10.1.1.5 52 80 120


I'm trying to code a program which prints the total number of packets based on same sending source port and same recieving destination port I want the output like:

Src Dst Srcport Destport Packets

10.1.1.1 10.1.1.2 50 5052 50

10.1.1.1 10.1.1.5 52 80 120

Below is script which i'm currently using.

import dpkt

from dpkt.ip import IP

from dpkt.ethernet import Ethernet

import struct

import socket

import csv

def ip_to_str(address):

return socket.inet_ntoa(address)

f = open('ptpv2.pcap', 'rb')

pcap = dpkt.pcap.Reader(f)

total_packet = 0

print "Source, Destination, Length, TTL, TOS, OFF, Protocol"

for ts, buf in pcap:

eth = dpkt.ethernet.Ethernet(buf)

total_packet += 1

if eth.type != dpkt.ethernet.ETH_TYPE_IP:

continue
ip = eth.data

do_not_fragment = bool(dpkt.ip.IP_DF)

more_fragments = bool(dpkt.ip.IP_MF)

fragment_offset = bool(dpkt.ip.IP_OFFMASK)

Source = "%s" % ip_to_str(ip.src)

Destination = "%s" % ip_to_str(ip.dst)

Length = "%d" % (ip.len)

TTL = "%d" % (ip.ttl)

OFF = ip.off

TOS = ip.tos

Protocol = ip.p

print Source, Destination, Length, TTL, TOS, OFF, Protocol

print "from %s to %s on port %s contains %s packets" %(Source, Destination, ip.data.dport)

I have done with the code for all the fields like print src, dst, Srcport, Destport from pcap but am not able to code for count packets based on same srcport and dstport for whole pcap or csv file.
I hope you understand what I really want,I'm stuck from many weeks, tried many tutorials, put the question on stack overflow but no response from 3 weeks,  not getting how to do this. Any help will be appreciated.

Looks like the proxy settings aren't being set in all the right places?

    password = self.proxy_config['password']
KeyError: u'password'
```

From what I've seen, upon launch Will starts 3 new processes - one each for the scheduler, XMPP, and web server (bottle). Normally on `KeyboardInterupt` or `SystemExit`, Will stops each of these threads cleanly.

I've been experimenting with running Will under [envconsul](https://github.com/hashicorp/envconsul), which watches and loads configs from the Consul KV store and inserts them into the ENV before starting Will. The trouble seems to be that when changes occur to the Consul KV data, envconsul sends a `SIGTERM` to Will, which ends up killing the original process, but not the three spawned processes. envconsul then restarts Will, which spawns 3 new processes, leaving the original three running.

I did take a hack at a patch but I'm crap at threading code, and have not yet made it work. Expect it will have to do with registering a `SIGTERM` handler (correctly) then shutting things down when it's fired.

issue_title: Bad hang then stalls and weirdness
issue_body:
### Step 1: Describe your environment

  * System (Intel/AMD/Raspberry Pi etc): Intel i7-855OU, X1 Carbon G6
  * OS version: Linux Mint 18.3 Cinnamon
  * s-tui version: Whatever the pip install gives you
  * Installation method(pip/PPA/source code): pip install
  
### Step 2: Describe the problem:
#### Observed Results:

  * What happened? (Traceback of a crash/Image/description):

First test: after running for a few seconds, and possibly dependent upon user keypresses, the program hung the whole system hard, such that I have to Raise Elephants. issue_title: bare integers don't work as keys
issue_body:
From the spec:

"Keys may be either bare or quoted. Bare keys may only contain letters, numbers, underscores, and dashes (A-Za-z0-9_-)."

Test case:

5="bruce"

Result:

pytoml.core.TomlError: script.bb(1, 1): unexpected

Reason:

By the time pytoml has gotten to the question of whether a token is an id, it has already checked to see if it is a datetime or a float; or failed back to int if it was neither a datetime or a float.

However, what it should do is instead check to see if there's an equals sign (possibly after some amount of whitespace) after the int, and if there is, mark it as an id.

issue_title: Tags/Releases missing after migration to GitHub
issue_body: I went to do a comparison of versions of six in GitHub today only to discover that there are no tags on the repository, though they were there in Mercurial. Would you please consider adding/pushing tags for the released versions?

I am getting started learning how to implement chempy into some of our metabolomics workflows and have hit an area where I need to learn more.  I'm looking for direction in how to best evaluate the output of balance_stoichiometry.

For this scenario:

```
balance_stoichiometry({u'C7H5O3-', u'O2', u'C21H27N7O14P2-2', u'H+'},
           {u'C7H5O4-', u'C21H26N7O14P2-', u'H2O'})
```

The following is returned:

```
({u'C21H27N7O14P2-2': x1, u'C7H5O3-': 2, u'H+': x1, u'O2': x1/2 + 1},
 {u'C21H26N7O14P2-': x1, u'C7H5O4-': 2, u'H2O': x1})
```

Looking at the dtypes, I see that they are SymPy types:

```
<class 'sympy.core.numbers.One'>
<class 'sympy.core.numbers.Integer'>
<class 'sympy.core.symbol.Symbol'>
```

And quickly heading over to SymPy, it seems that there is a great deal of things you can do regarding SymPy expressions, evaluations, and substitutions.

I'm looking to convert these coefficients into a SymPy expression and find the solution that satisfies the requirement that all coefficients are integers.  My naive idea is to make a SymPy expression and eval it for x1:=[1:Inf] until equality.

In the example above, x1 would be equal to 2.

I'm guessing that you have an excellent way to do this that is better than my idea, but its not immediately coming to me.  Any help is much appreciated.

For FreeBSD, I added this patch:
```
-elif _platform.system() == 'Linux':
+elif _platform.system() == 'Linux' or _platform.system() == 'FreeBSD':
```
But you should allow the whole set of BSDs: FreeBSD/OpenBSD/NetBSD/FragonflyBSD.

There may be something wrong with Espresso:

I am trying to include a big table from a file in my markdown document, which I want to appear in a landscape (the rest of the document uses portrait) using [pdflscape](https://ctan.org/pkg/pdflscape). I am having  a couple of issues.
1- The output is not correct, a string of YAML appears where the table should be (without landscape the table works fine).
2- It takes really long to compile the document.

Is there a better way to make big tables appear in landscape?

EDIT: I solved the problem by including a tag in the the .md file and then converting to .tex, and then replacing the tag by the appropriate LaTeX syntax. My  compilation script currently looks like this:
```bash
#!/bin/sh
FILE_NAME="report"
pandoc -s -N -F pantable "$FILE_NAME.md" -o "$FILE_NAME.tex"
sed -i 's/begin{figure}\[htbp\]/begin{figure}\[H\]/g' "$FILE_NAME.tex"
sed -i 's/!beginlandscape/\\begin{landscape}/g' "$FILE_NAME.tex"
sed -i 's/!endlandscape/\\end{landscape}/g' "$FILE_NAME.tex"
pdflatex "$FILE_NAME.tex" -0 "$FILE_NAME.pdf" > /dev/null
rm "$FILE_NAME.tex"
rm "$FILE_NAME.log"
rm "$FILE_NAME.out"
rm "$FILE_NAME.aux"
```

issue_title: Is it possible to use `plot_decision_regions` function with model fit on a Pandas DataFrame?
issue_body:
question originally posted at: https://stackoverflow.com/q/49167961/1870832
reproduced below...

With `plot_decision_regions`, `X` and `y` have to be numpy arrays, and `clf` needs to have a `predict()` method. My problem is that in my case, the model `clf` object I would like to visualize has already been fitted on a Pandas DataFrame...

```
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import xgboost as xgb

import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')
from mlxtend.plotting import plot_decision_regions
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


# Create arbitrary dataset for example
df = pd.DataFrame({'Planned_End': np.random.uniform(low=-5, high=5, size=50),
                   'Actual_End':  np.random.uniform(low=-1, high=1, size=50),
                   'Late':        np.random.random_integers(low=0,  high=2, size=50)}
)

# Fit a Classifier to the data
# This classifier is fit on the data as a Pandas DataFrame
X = df[['Planned_End', 'Actual_End']]
y = df['Late']

clf = xgb.XGBClassifier()
clf.fit(X, y)

```
So now when I try to use plot_decision_regions passing X/y as numpy arrays...

```
# Plot Decision Region using mlxtend's awesome plotting function
plot_decision_regions(X=X.values,
                      y=y.values,
                      clf=clf,
                      legend=2)

```
I (understandably) get an error that the model can't find the column names of the dataset it was trained on

```
ValueError: feature_names mismatch: ['Planned_End', 'Actual_End'] ['f0', 'f1']
expected Planned_End, Actual_End in input data
training data did not have the following fields: f1, f0

```
In my actual case, it would be a big deal to avoid training our model on Pandas DataFrames. Is there a way to still produce decision_regions plots for a classifier trained on a Pandas DataFrame?

By default, Python ignores certain warnings to avoid generating excessive noise. Unfortunately, this means that useful warnings such as DeprecationWarnings are likely to be overlooked. 

A test runner is one of the most useful places for them to be visible.  Indeed, the default unittest TestRunner [does show them even if they would be hidden by default](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.html#unittest.TextTestRunner). 

It's possible to get them by running `python -W d -m green`, but this also displays all sorts of other warnings that don't have anything to do with the code under test. 

IMHO Green should follow unittest lead and enable extra warnings by default.

I then pass a list of these `ObjectIdentity`'s to a `pysnmp.entity.rfc3413.oneliner.cmdgen.CommandGenerator.getCmd` with the kwarg `lookupMib` set to False.
However, this code path still seems to trigger some MIB resolution to resolve names, which is taking a big performance hit.

Is there some simple way to get get an OID without engaging any kind of MIB loading or resolution?

issue_body:
So I have been battling this for quite a while now... The preprocessor works fine on the tweets takes from a text file ('str' format), but problems start when I take tweets directly from Twitter using Twitter API search. They are initially in unicode and contain all sorts of characters including, for example, emojis. 

If a tweet contains only text, then it is being parsed without any problems. However, if it contains emojis or other weird characters, I get a Unicode Encode Error. 
The parser is supposed to deal with emojis, so I am struggling to understand what is happening. I also can't encode the tweet as ASCII because ASCII has no representation of the new emojis. Or am I wrong somewhere?
Thanks in advance!

issue_title: Config Parameter "enable" is acting in the wrong scope
issue_body:
See the wiki page: https://github.com/RussBaz/enforce/wiki/Configuration

In a nutshell, the built in functionality with wrapt lets us disable these checks and skip over them, however since this happens at the bytecode level it needs to occur right as the file is read.

This behavior isn't ideal, as we would like to be able to dynamically disable functions based on possibly arguments, or other things. This currently isn't possible...

This is a low priority issue, however we still need to keep track of it. (I'll try to get to it soon, but if someone beats me to it that would also be great.)

Hello @etingof,

First of all, thank you for creating SNMPSim and supporting it. We are currently using your tool to simulate Cisco and Xirrus Access Points. We used the snmprec functionality to create .snmprec files based on real Cisco and Xirrus devices and it worked perfectly.

Since we needed to simulate SNMPSet behaviour, I read SNMPSim's documentation regarding varation modules and since I picked the writecache module. Editing the snmprecs generated from the Access Points, I changed all OIDs I was interested to edit according to the tutorial to be like this:

1.3.2.4.23.3|2:writecache|value=27    for example.

However, I noticed that the writecache flag seems to be incompatible with encoded datatypes. For example, some Xirrus OIDs contain encoded strings and have the '4x' datatype instead of simply '4.
When SNMPSim tries to parse '4x:writecache' it throws an error about invalid type. Consequently, I added 'writecache' only to non-encoded OIDs.

Is there any way to use the writecache for encoded OIDs or should I resort to other solutions such as MySQL backed storage.

Finally, I attempted to create a new Tunnel on my simulated Xirrus device. To do this, I have to add new OIDs while the simulation is running that reflect the tunnel creation on the device. I can easily do this by editing the .snmprec file and SNMPSim will detect it. However, this is not a desirable solution in our case and I would like to know if there are other ways in which I can instruct SNMPSim to create new OIDs and then fetch them via SNMPGet. Will a MySQL backend once again help here?

issue_title: --indent-size option doesn't apply to dictionaries, lists and tuples
issue_body:
The `--indent-size` option doesn't seem to apply to dictionaries, lists and tuples. Here's some example input:

```
[
        'foo',
        'bar'
]

{
        'foo': 'bar',
        'baz': 42
}

(
        1,
        2,
        3
)

def test():
        pass
```

Running `autopep8 --indent-size 8` on the input above processes the following result:

```
[
    'foo',
    'bar'
]

{
    'foo': 'bar',
    'baz': 42
}

(
    1,
    2,
    3
)


def test():
        pass
```

As you can see, the indentation on the dictionary, the tuple and the list was corrected to 4 spaces despite the `--indent-size` option being set to `8`. I wanted to submit a PR, but I couldn't find the part of the code which takes care of this.

The Juniper methods do not implement `check_config_mode()`.  This causes unexpected behavior and unnecessary duplication of code when trying to implement other features.  The current `config_mode()` method in the Juniper file, for example, could be eliminated and a `config_mode(config_command="configure")` (from **ssh_connection.py**) could be used instead.  I can work on this later, but here are a few notes.

The existing `check_config_mode()` method should be usable like this: `check_config_mode(check_string='[edit')`.  That should enable certain methods to be written like this:

```lang-py
def config_mode(self):
    '''
    First check whether currently already in configuration mode.

    Enter config mode (if necessary)
    '''

    if not self.check_config_mode(check_string='[edit'):
        output += self.send_command('configure\n', strip_prompt=False, strip_command=False)
        if 'unknown command' in output:
            raise ValueError("Failed to enter configuration mode")
        if not '[edit' in output:
            raise ValueError("Failed to enter configuration mode")

    return output
```

Other methods could be written as well to take advantage of the `check_config_mode()` method, such as `exit_config_mode()`.  `commit()` could probably be rewritten to use `check_config_mode()` as well as it doesn't make much sense to check to see if you're in config mode, then enter config mode if you're not, then commit the config.  That's typically not a valid workflow, and there typically isn't a use case for committing nothing.  Maybe there's one I haven't considered, though.

The `LocalTZInfo` and `USTimeZone` functions do not support the PEP495 `fold` attribute, and will thus be wrong around ambiguous datetimes. See:

```
from dateutil import tz
from boltons.timeutils import LocalTZInfo, Eastern

from datetime import datetime

def fmt_offset(dt):
    # Default timedelta formatting is ugly
    td = dt.utcoffset()
    fmt_str = ('-' if td < timedelta(0) else '') + '{}'
    return fmt_str.format(abs(td))

EASTERN_DU = tz.gettz('US/Eastern')
LOCAL_DU = tz.tzlocal()

EASTERN_BO = Eastern
LOCAL_BO = LocalTZInfo()

dt_unfolded = datetime(2011, 11, 6, 1, 30)
dt_folded = tz.enfold(dt_unfolded, fold=1)

for tz_du, tz_bo in ((EASTERN_DU, EASTERN_BO), (LOCAL_DU, LOCAL_BO)):
    dt_du_u = dt_unfolded.replace(tzinfo=tz_du)
    dt_bo_u = dt_unfolded.replace(tzinfo=tz_bo)

    dt_du_f = dt_folded.replace(tzinfo=tz_du)
    dt_bo_f = dt_folded.replace(tzinfo=tz_bo)

    print("(Dateutil, folded)   Offset: {} TZName: {}".format(fmt_offset(dt_du_f),
                                                              dt_du_f.tzname()))
    print("(boltons, folded)    Offset: {} TZName: {}".format(fmt_offset(dt_bo_f),
                                                             dt_bo_f.tzname()))
    print()

    print("(Dateutil, unfolded) Offset: {} TZName: {}".format(fmt_offset(dt_du_u),
                                                                dt_du_u.tzname()))
    print("(boltons, unfolded)  Offset: {} TZName: {}".format(fmt_offset(dt_bo_u),
                                                               dt_bo_u.tzname()))
    print()

###
### Result:
"""
(Dateutil, folded)   Offset: -5:00:00 TZName: EST
(boltons, folded)    Offset: -5:00:00 TZName: EST

(Dateutil, unfolded) Offset: -4:00:00 TZName: EDT
(boltons, unfolded)  Offset: -5:00:00 TZName: EST

(Dateutil, folded)   Offset: -5:00:00 TZName: EST
(boltons, folded)    Offset: -4:00:00 TZName: EDT

(Dateutil, unfolded) Offset: -4:00:00 TZName: EDT
(boltons, unfolded)  Offset: -4:00:00 TZName: EDT
"""
```

It believe it's slightly easier to fix this if you only support the built-in fold attribute in Python 3.6, otherwise you need to add a backport `_DatetimeWithFold` object. 

[Here](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/tz/_common.py#L31) is how that is handled in `dateutil`, [here](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/tz/tz.py#L159) is dateutil's implementation of `tzlocal` (some of the fold handling happens in the parent class). I don't have a ready-made `USTimeZone` implementation, but I believe the example in the Python documentation was updated accordingly.

Note that `dateutil` is BSD-licensed, so taking code from there should be compatible with this project.

www@nas:~$ rtorrent -h

    raise Fault(**self._stack[0])

В rtorrent 0.9.6 относительно 0.9.4 поменялся синтаксис команд, и старые команды вроде как  [автоматически должны преобразовываться в новые](https://github.com/rakshasa/rtorrent/blob/master/src/main.cc#L591), но это не так :) Конфиг rtorrent после апгрейда на 0.9.6 надо было править руками, да и диагностика проблемы у таких как я не-линуксоидов заняла порядочно времени.

Как я понял, rtorrent 0.9.6 через XMLRPC отвечает, что не знает метода **set_xmlrpc_size_limit**. В rtorrent 0.9.6 (и, надеюсь, далее) этот метод называется **network.xmlrpc.size_limit.set**.
Required for v1, to prevent anyone from running it.

issue_body:
Continuing the last thought...

Without options we get a summary of information and an ISO 8601 date.

```
%watermark

2016-01-31T21:17:31

CPython 3.4.4

compiler   : GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-1)

If we add extra options, we lose the ISO 8601 date-time (and just have the date):
```
%watermark -d -m -v -p numpy,matplotlib -g

2016-01-31 

CPython 3.4.4
IPython 4.0.1

numpy 1.10.2
matplotlib 1.5.0

Could we either have a new flag for an ISO 8601 date, perhaps ```-i``` which uses the same new ```strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S')```?

I am working on adding support for a 'multi page' app, that would allow for defining routes to different spyre "apps".

You can check out my progress at my fork/master. I've basically got it working. I've got a draft of an example app (that is the example_show_all_the_inputs.py, with 2 additional app classes that are basically exact copies of the first.) At the bottom you can see what I've got for how you would use this functionality.

I added an additional class in server.py that I'm calling Site, that takes the top node as a required argument and exposes a methods to add a new page/route, to change the top node (which would be somewhat dangerous, but there might be a use case to do so), and to launch the site.

I'm not really happy with the naming conventions, as I'm calling the top node the "root" node, which is a little confusing because the object is a server.Root object. Anyone have any input on a better naming convention for a page/route/node and for the top of the page/node/route tree?

There is also an issue with this setup that I think has something to do with how the spinning_wheel is implemented. While the spinning wheel is active the terminal throws a warning/error "QPixmap: It is not safe to use pixmaps outside the GUI thread". My guess is that whatever is being used to determine when the spinning wheel should be replaced by a plot or HTML element is causing this error. It's not strictly causing any problems, but it's probably something that would need to be figured out before incorporating this kind of functionality into your master.

In general what are your thoughts on this?

In terms of how this might be used, I'm not sure if there is anything that could/should be done to the template to allow better use of a multipage site (A navbar across the top? An easy ability to make the control panel buttons be links? an output template/function?). It would be easy enough to just make an HTML output block that would have links, but I'm just wondering if this is something that maybe could be supported better by updating the view.html template.

issue_title: saveas does not work with non-bed output - bug or feature?
issue_body:
I was doing some `groupby` calculations returning a two column results, but saving them with `.saveas()` returns an error:

> Traceback (most recent call last):
>   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 668, in decorated
>     result = method(self, *args, **kwargs)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 2729, in saveas
>     fn = self._collapse(self, fn=fn, trackline=trackline)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 1097, in _collapse
>     for i in iterable:
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 924, in __iter__
>     elif self.file_type == 'sam':
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 857, in file_type
>     self._file_type = IntervalFile(self.fn).file_type
>   File "pybedtools/cbedtools.pyx", line 694, in pybedtools.cbedtools.IntervalFile.file_type (pybedtools/cbedtools.cpp:10001)
>   File "pybedtools/cbedtools.pyx", line 695, in pybedtools.cbedtools.IntervalFile.file_type (pybedtools/cbedtools.cpp:9954)
>   File "pybedtools/cbedtools.pyx", line 529, in pybedtools.cbedtools.create_interval_from_list (pybedtools/cbedtools.cpp:7272)
> IndexError: list index out of range

Saving the result with `output=` works fine. My guess is that `.saveas()` tries to validate the output as the bed file, but `output=` doesn't (sorry couldn't find the source code to see if this is correct). Here is a reproducible example:

```python
# Python 2.7.6 (default, Jun 22 2015, 17:58:13) 
# [GCC 4.8.2] on linux2
# Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

import pybedtools
# pybedtools 0.6.9

a = pybedtools.example_bedtool('gdc.gff')
b = pybedtools.example_bedtool('gdc.bed')
c = a.intersect(b, c=True)
print 
d = c.groupby(g=[1, 4, 5], c=10, ops=['sum'])
print d
## works
d.saveas('test1.tmp')

## now 2 col output
print c.groupby(g=[3], c=10, ops=['sum'])
## fails
c.groupby(g=[3], c=10, ops=['sum']).saveas('test2.tmp')
## works
c.groupby(g=[3], c=10, ops=['sum'], output='test2.tmp')
``` 

This might be a feature, but I couldn't find it in the docs.

Cheers

issue_title: Process cmdline is truncated on Solaris
issue_body:
`p.cmdline()` is truncated to 80 characters on Solaris 11. `/proc/PID/psinfo` that psutil uses to read the command line contains only the beginning - See e.g. https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/3934/how-can-i-get-a-full-process-list-in-solaris-without-truncated-lines.
The solution would be to use the same method as `pargs` tool - https://github.com/illumos/illumos-gate/blob/09f79f7c66b85f056db11f58210dc6182c9b1aef/usr/src/cmd/ptools/pargs/pargs.c. It's not however clear to me how can it be easily ported as it's using `libproc`, which is documented in https://github.com/illumos/illumos-gate/blob/09f79f7c66b85f056db11f58210dc6182c9b1aef/usr/src/lib/libproc/common/libproc.h as:
```
 * In the meantime, be aware that any program linked with libproc in this
 * release of Solaris is almost guaranteed to break in the next release.
 *
 * In short, do not use this header file or libproc for any purpose.
```

As a workaround `cmdline()` could try to launch `pargs` if available but it bears a `fork` cost.

Didn't test any other Solaris version.

Invalid: https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil#further-process-apis
```
import psutil
for proc in psutil.process_iter(attrs=['pid', 'name']):
    print(proc.info)
```

Ok: https://pythonhosted.org/psutil/#psutil.process_iter
```
import psutil

for proc in psutil.process_iter():
    try:
        pinfo = proc.as_dict(attrs=['pid', 'name'])
    except psutil.NoSuchProcess:
        pass
    else:
        print(pinfo)
```

issue_title: BSD: disk_partitions(all=False) always return an empty list
issue_body: On BSD systems there is no difference between `df` and `df -a`. The same partitions are always returned. As such psutil should ignore the `all` argument passed to `disk_partitions` as there simply is no way to distinguish between physical partitions and "others". We'll just always return them all.

    2018-01-04 13:30:55+00:00
    
    .TIME_ZONE='UTC', USE_TZ=False
    2018-01-04 22:30:55
    2018-01-04 22:30:55
    
    .TIME_ZONE='Asia/Seoul', USE_TZ=True
    2018-01-04 22:30:55
    2018-01-04 13:30:55+00:00
    
    .TIME_ZONE='Asia/Seoul', USE_TZ=False
    2018-01-04 22:30:55
    2018-01-04 22:30:55

The strange part is `test_freeze_time1()`, which tests under `TIME_ZONE='UTC', USE_TZ=True`, the reason that I think it strange is that `datetime.now()` doesn't print out `UTC` time, which is not True if I removed `@freeze_time("2018-01-04 13:30:55", tz_offset=9)` : 


    from django.test.utils import override_settings
    
    class TimeZoneTest(TestCase):
        
        @override_settings(TIME_ZONE='UTC', USE_TZ=True)
        def test_freeze_time1(self):
            print("TIME_ZONE='UTC', USE_TZ=True")
            print(datetime.now())
            print(timezone.now())
    
    
        @override_settings(TIME_ZONE='UTC', USE_TZ=False)
        def test_freeze_time2(self):
            print("TIME_ZONE='UTC', USE_TZ=False")
            print(datetime.now())
            print(timezone.now())
    
    
        @override_settings(TIME_ZONE='Asia/Seoul', USE_TZ=True)
        def test_freeze_time3(self):
            print("TIME_ZONE='Asia/Seoul', USE_TZ=True")
            print(datetime.now())
            print(timezone.now())
    
    
        @override_settings(TIME_ZONE='Asia/Seoul', USE_TZ=False)
        def test_freeze_time4(self):
            print("TIME_ZONE='Asia/Seoul', USE_TZ=False")
            print(datetime.now())
            print(timezone.now())

**Result**

    TIME_ZONE='UTC', USE_TZ=True
    2018-02-13 09:10:49.715005
    2018-02-13 09:10:49.715018+00:00
    
    .TIME_ZONE='UTC', USE_TZ=False
    2018-02-13 09:10:49.715970
    2018-02-13 09:10:49.715980
    
    .TIME_ZONE='Asia/Seoul', USE_TZ=True
    2018-02-13 18:10:49.716758
    2018-02-13 09:10:49.716769+00:00
    
    .TIME_ZONE='Asia/Seoul', USE_TZ=False
    2018-02-13 18:10:49.717475
    2018-02-13 18:10:49.717486

As you can see here, both print out `UTC` time under `TIME_ZONE='UTC', USE_TZ=True` condition.

The other settings seem work well.

Or, Did I miss something?

Hi folks,

I have a util (say dateoverrider) that freezes time to a particular date (say X). I have imported this utill across my code base, so that the faketime is used on every datetime operation. 
At some point in the code, i want to go back to my original date (say O). I wrote a util (say daterestter), to stop freezer. But at this point of time, freeze_time method expects a datetime object. I don't know how to proceed from here on since my fakedate (X) is not in scope.
I tried a very stupid way of starting freeze time again with fake time, and stopping it twice. This returned my original date. But, on importing my dateoverrider util again, the freezer doesn't start.
Below is my code:

**Util dateoverrider**
from freezegun import freeze_time
freezer = freeze_time(fake_date)
freezer.start()


**Util daterestter**

from freezegun import freeze_time

freezer = freeze_time(fake_date)
freezer.start()
freezer.stop()
freezer.stop()


P.S:  I'm trying to go back to my orignal date due to pandas package. It throws me an error saying 'cannot import Nat/hashtable'.  On using my restter util, i'm able to overcome this error. The flow is as shown below.
import overrider
import resetter
import pandas
import overrider
The above flow doesnt return me error. But on importing my overrider util again, the fake time is not set.


Please correct me wherever i'm going wrong. Alternative solutions are always welcome. Please suggest

Thanks a ton,
Thilak.

issue_title: Not really an issue, but is there a configuration to limit the number of commands the bot can handle per minute?

issue_body:
I do have 
```python
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
@client.event
async def on_error(event, *args, **kwargs):
    print("Oh no, error!")

@client.event
async def on_message(message):
    loop.create_task(handle_command(message))

@client.event
async def handle_command(msg):
    x=5/0
```


As you can see, there is an exception in `handle_command`. This exception does not trigger `on_error` function. It outputs error to console.

What am I doing wrong?

issue_title: Question: Get user status? (Online, Idle, Do Not Disturb)
issue_body: Is there a way to get the user status? (the green/yellow/red/grey circle) I can see a way to change the bots status, but nothing about referencing a users status.

Please, add support for Python 2.5 or give an instruction how do that.

P.S.: target is mobile phones With PyS60 2.0.0 support
* https://garage.maemo.org/projects/pys60/
  * https://garage.maemo.org/frs/?group_id=854

I don't know if the Discord Gateway provides this, but a new listener that would be triggered when a new audit logs entry is created would be great, so we can listen to all types of actions, with the author, target, reason...

issue_title: Schema properties documented as list, but dict can be more suitable
issue_body:
[Here](https://github.com/axnsan12/drf-yasg/blob/master/src/drf_yasg/openapi.py#L428), Schema's `properties` is documented as being a list of child Schemas and SchemaRefs. issue_title: Add option to write cert to file
issue_body:
acme-tiny should offer an option (-o or --out) to write the obtained certificate to a file upon success, instead of writing to the standard output. 

For reference, the current documented usage is as follows:
`python acme_tiny.py --account-key ./account.key --csr ./domain.csr --acme-dir /usr/share/nginx/html/.well-known/acme-challenge/ > signed.crt`

The current workflow (writing to stdout and letting the user redirect to a file) is problematic for automated generations of certificates. 
When using the example command, signed.crt is (over)written even when the generation fails. 
If the cert generation fails while trying to update a existing cert, the existing cert will be overwritten and lost. 

I realize the user could workaround this (redirecting to temp file, then somehow verify that the file is a valid cert,  then replacing the old cert), but it would be much easier to just support the option in acme-tiny

issue_title: When exporting to StatsD - Uptime values loop after 1 day
issue_body:
#### Description

When exporting to StatsD/Graphite, the Uptime values being returned by Glances do not reflect the ones in the UI.  Instead they reset to 0 every 24 hours.

I believe this is caused by the use of the seconds property on the timedelta.  In glances_uptime.py, in the  "get_export" function self.uptime.seconds is returned. For timedelta this the seconds part of the difference, but I believe the totalseconds() method should be used to return the whole difference in seconds. 

From the timedelta help():
 **|  total_seconds(...)
 |      Total seconds in the duration.**
 |  
 |  ----------------------------------------------------------------------
 |  Data descriptors defined here:
 |  
 |  days
 |      Number of days.
 |  
 |  microseconds
 |      Number of microseconds (>= 0 and less than 1 second).
 |  
 **|  seconds
 |      Number of seconds (>= 0 and less than 1 day).**

#### Versions

* Glances (glances -V): 2.9.1
* PSutil (glances -V): 5.2.1
* Operating System (lsb_release -a):

issue_title: Default alerts for io wait on CPU plugin seem wrong
issue_body:
#### Description

IO Wait on Linux is a form of idle time. Its an indication that the processor is idle while one or more process is doing I/O. It doesn't necessarily indicate a problem, unless that I/O is itself taking a long time. This is a good description of it:

https://blog.pregos.info/wp-content/uploads/2010/09/iowait.txt

Given this information, I'm not sure that the following default configuration of the CPU plugin makes sense:

```
iowait_careful=50
iowait_warning=70
iowait_critical=90
```

On a very fast mostly idle system, writing to rotational disks often causes Glances to produce critical CPU_IOWAIT alerts, simply because writing to rotational disks is slow and the CPU is doing nothing else at the time. These alerts don't actually mean anything is wrong though.

#### Versions

```
# glances -V
Glances v2.7.1 with psutil v5.0.1
```

* Operating System (lsb_release -a):

I'm just about to release the first version of [mitmproxy](http://github.com/mitmproxy/mitmproxy) with config parsing based on ConfigArgParse. Thanks a bunch for your solid work. 

Something we'd find immensely useful is the ability to specify values in key/value pairs as Python strings and triple-quoted multi-line strings, with Python-style escape sequences. This would make the config files for our tools hugely more powerful.

This would require converting the current parser to something more sophisticated. [PyParsing](http://pyparsing.wikispaces.com/) has built-in definitions for quoted strings, and implementing the parser in it would not be very much work.

I don't have the bandwidth for this myself, but please let me know if a patch like this would be pulled, and I may get to it some time down the track.

The `rules` flag can be really useful, but I noticed that filenames are not verified, so a rule to match `.htpasswd` wouldn't be part of a found regex. In some cases, it is easier to detect potential secrets using the filename instead of its contents. 

Would it be possible to include this feature? By the way, I modified truffleHog to check filenames with a conditional `elif secret_regexes[key].findall(blob.a_blob.name)` in [regex_check](https://github.com/dxa4481/truffleHog/blob/dev/truffleHog/truffleHog.py#L190) and added a `try` statement to catch exceptions when `a_blob` doesn't have a `name` property, but there are probably better ways to do it.

issue_title: Please add support for /usr/bin/editor on Debian
issue_body:
In Debian, we have a system called [alternatives](https://wiki.debian.org/DebianAlternatives) which allows the user to select the default applications for several tasks, including the editor. This system provides an `/usr/bin/editor` symlink pointing to user's command-line editor of choice. It would be nice if you could add support for this.

Our [Policy](https://www.debian.org/doc/debian-policy/ch-customized-programs.html#s11.4) recommends the following algorithm:
> Thus, every program that launches an editor or pager must use the `EDITOR` or `PAGER` environment variable to determine the editor or pager the user wishes to use. If these variables are not set, the programs `/usr/bin/editor` and `/usr/bin/pager` should be used, respectively.

issue_title: Need a way to define data type/length when passing variables in SQL Server
issue_body:
I'm doing selects/inserts/updates on my tables and SQL Server is creating an execution plan for every permutation of character lengths that the selects/inserts/updates into the table from python.

I believe this is causing some issues with the performance of the db and may create some memory issues.

I would like to be able to set the data type/length when passing parameters. Ideally, pyodbc would know the datatype/length before inserting and set them accordingly but I don't know if this will create more issues issues than it will solve, so being able to define them myself might help.

More specifically, when I run a trace, this is what I see:

```
declare @p1 int
set @p1=4882452
exec sp_prepexec @p1 output,N'@P1 nvarchar(11),@P2 nvarchar(16)',N'SELECT TOP 1 C_KEY FROM C WHERE A_NM = @P1 AND C_NM = @P2',N'Zantesuken1',N'RasalhagueLegacy'
select @p1
```

and when looking at the execution plans, I find that an execution plan has been created for every single permutation of these selects.

Perhaps something like this:

cursor.execute("SELECT X FROM Y WHERE Z1 = ? AND Z2 = ?", param1.type(varchar(50)), param2.type(bigint))

No idea if this makes sense and maybe this is already possible somehow and I don't know about it, so let me know if there's a way around this issue.

*edit* Also noticed that the auto-incrementing @p1 seems to be going up really quickly (I'm doing a lot of transactions). Is there any way to reset this counter? I've tried deleting the cursor, closing the connection, but nothing seems to be working.

issue_title: Maybe allow a few more obvious shorthands for semver specs
issue_body:
Spinoff of #45.

Noticed that e.g. in a situation where one puts out a minor release and major release at the same time, one doesn't actually want features in the minor to duplicate into the major all the time - it's implied the major will include them.

So, one wants to do e.g. `(>=1.17,<2.0)`, explicitly. However, as it's a feature and thus only subject to landing in the one minor release bucket, one can more succinctly say `(==1.17)`.

Thus this ticket is for making that even easier and perhaps allowing `(1.17)`, i.e. the `==` is implicit. Not 100% sure, and assuming folks will only really want to use these syntaxes around (rare) major releases, it's not like we really need the savings. But noting just in case.

issue_title: Example of compiling app modules using Cython compiler
issue_body:
https://github.com/cython/cython

Packagers like PyInstaller, Py2exe and cx_Freeze can package app into an executable, however they do not provide good security for source codes. Sources when using these packagers are protected by compiling them to bytecode, but that bytecode can be easily converted into original almost complete source codes with only code comments removed. All variables, modules, strings, everything can be seen as in original code. PyInstaller provides an additional security by setting a secret cipher that allows to encrypt bytecode compiled code, however this is only security by obscurity and can be easily broken as well.

The only viable options to really protect sources codes are:
1. Use Cython to compile app modules into .pyd/.so modules (real binaries) and only then package app using e.g. PyInstaller.
2. Use Nuitka compiler which can both protect sources and also package app into executable (Issue #396).

See also related issues with [packaging](https://github.com/cztomczak/cefpython/labels/packaging) label.

Hello!

As part of packaging this for Debian, I found a license incompatibility (and a mismarking!).

The file `ntlm_auth/des_c.py`, `ntlm_auth/des.py`, and `ntlm_auth/des_data.py` are copyright Dmitry A. Rozmanov and licensed it as part of the NTLMAPS project under the GPLv2.  The header marking shows that it's LGPLv3+, which is incorrect.

Unfortunately, GPLv2 (aka GPLv2 only) isn't compatible with LGPLv3+.  If you hold the copyright to all other code, you could relicense the entire project under the LGPLv2.1+, which would effectively cause it to be distributed under GPLv2 itself.  The other option would be removing those files from the project and replacing them.

Please let me know.

issue_title: A complex generator of a lot of dates.
issue_body:
Sometimes we need to generate a lot of `datetime` objects and at this moment we have not a good solution for this, so I suggest something like this:

```python
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def bulk_create_datetimes(diapason=(), *args, **kwargs):
    dt_objects = []
    # Maybe that's better to raise an exception 
    # instead of using default range?
    try:
        start, end = diapason
    except ValueError:
        end = datetime.now()
        start = end - timedelta(days=7)

    while start <= end:
        start += timedelta(*args, **kwargs)
        dt_objects.append(start)
    return dt_objects
```

How it works:

```python
>>> week_ago = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=7)
>>> now = datetime.now()
>>> datetimes = bulk_create_datetimes(diapason=(week_ago, now), minutes=60)
>>> for i in datetimes:
....    print(i)
```
Result:
```
2018-08-21 12:58:55.687991
2018-08-21 13:58:55.687991
2018-08-21 14:58:55.687991
2018-08-21 15:58:55.687991
2018-08-21 16:58:55.687991
...
2018-08-28 12:58:55.687991
```

So, I think that it's pretty simple to understand and there is no need for comments.

That gives extreme flexibility in generating dates because a user can generate dates with any difference between them (including seconds, minutes and etc.).
We should check every points: http://python.apichecklist.com

issue_title: Creos Smart Meters P1 data encrypted
issue_body:
Hi,

Trying to find out if the Luxembourg smart meter which has a P1 port, can be integrated into Home Assistant.

In their [specifications](https://smarty.creos.net/wp-content/uploads/P1PortSpecification.pdf)  , they say the following:

> P1 protocol is a plain text protocol: it is required to secure the link to ensure end user data confidentiality. In deviation to DSMR P1, an encryption layer is added over the P1 protocol. This encryption layer is based on DLMS security suite 0 algorithm: AES128-GCM

Does your library support the decryption ?

issue_title: server only serves chunks in forward direction
issue_body:
This has been introduced in 04e7dbe9, as a fix for peer discovery, when misconfigured servers returned a mixture of testnet and mainnet servers to the client.

The client now requests a checkpoint (default checkpoint is the genesis block) when it initiates a connection, and it closes the connection if the returned header fails to pass the checkpoint. Therefore this fix is not needed anymore. 

Clients may need to request chunks in reverse order if there is a hard fork and they want to switch to the other branch. Commit 04e7dbe9 prevents them from fetching the other branch.

issue_title: Watchdog detects changes half a second later than dsoprea/PyInotify under Linux
issue_body:
I am working on a project where the file changes should be detected as soon as possible.
I started out using watchdog since it's crossplatform and conveniently abstracts the filesystem events,
but I noticed that watchdog takes 0.5s longer than [dsoprea/PyInotify](https://github.com/dsoprea/PyInotify) to detect file changes. Is this just the price you have to pay for the abstraction? Or could watchdog be more optimized?

Code I used for benchmarking:
```python
import time
import watchdog.events
import watchdog.observers

class EventHandler(watchdog.events.FileSystemEventHandler):
	def on_any_event(self, event):
		print(time.time())

observer = watchdog.observers.Observer()
observer.schedule(EventHandler(), '.', recursive=True)
observer.start()
input()
```

```python
import time
import inotify.adapters

i = inotify.adapters.InotifyTree(b'.')

for event in i.event_gen():
	if event and 'IN_CLOSE_WRITE' in event[1]:
		print(time.time())
```

At the moment, statement objects have an `extra_data` attribute designed to allow additional information to be stored on the statement object. This is useful for allowing logic adapters to cache calculated information on the statement.

The issue with the current setup is that having this as a dynamic field may not be the best way to store this data for multiple reasons. Mainly, No-SQL databases like Mongo DB work great with a dynamic field attributes, but SQL databases make it a bit more difficult to accomplish the same functionality.

The addition of the ability to set different caching backends for ChatterBot may be the most elegant solution. A key-value store for statements could provide an efficient way to maintain additional data associated with a statement. This change would also make ChatterBot more scalable by allowing it to integrate with key value stores like redis. Simple in-memory or file storage cache backends could also be made available for testing.

This may also be a better alternative to resolving #286.

Is it possible to get the n amount of best results? I'm using ChatterBot for autocomplete suggestions for a chat system and it would be great to have something that returns several potential answers instead of just the "Best" one.

issue_title: Default shard is always used, even when a different one is given.
issue_body:
I have been using your wrapper to play around with the API and I believe this is a bug.

say you do this:
`api = PUBG(myApiKey, 'xbox-na')`
`players = api.players_from_names('shroud', 'pc-na')`

it will fail to find shroud because it is still using 'xbox-na' shard for the url.

I've looked through the source code a bit and believe it has to do with this line:
`url = SHARD_URL + `**self.shard**` + "/players"`

within the coreapi players function. (and other endpoints as well)

it always uses the `self.shard` and not the passed in shard value. I feel like you are missing a single `shard = shard or self.shard` right before the _check_shard, and then using just  `shard` instead while building the url.


 

Sorry if I knew how to create a pull request with the suggested fixes I would.

Thank you very much for providing your PUBG API wrapper.

That is all.  

Thanks

issue_title: [Help Wanted] - Memoizing when modifying similar subparts.
issue_body:
Hi,
The lenses library was invaluable to helping write the initial prototype of my sequential circuit library 
[pyAiger](https://github.com/mvcisback/py-aiger). I mainly used the `lens` api to perform very recursive updates, for example, to turn latches into inputs:

```python
circ = bind(self).Recur(LatchIn) \
                 .Filter(lambda x: x.name in latches) \
                 .modify(lambda x: Input(name))
```

Unfortunately, due to scaling issues I've had to rewrite a number of methods. The key problem is that the same sub-circuit appears multiple times in the data structure. Thus, the `Recur` lens goes down the same path multiple times. My new hard-coded traversals are similarly recursive, but memoize to avoid going down the same path. How could I tell `python-lenses` to do this? For reference, this datastructure is essentially a forest with many of the trees overlapping. Is the best way to do this to memoize the `setattr` hook?

issue_title: "files" parameter is very flexible, but is underdocumented
issue_body:
I wanted to upload a file and set the remote filename, and I was happy to discover this bit of documentation:

https://internetarchive.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#setting-remote-filenames

Cool! But wait, I'm passing a dict to the `upload` function? I thought that took either a file object or a list of paths? Better double check the docs. Hm, the docs for that say:

> The filepaths or file-like objects to upload. This value can be an iterable or a single file-like object or string.

So, I guess this means it can also be a dict. I like the flexibility here, but we need to document it, and test the combinations:

1. file_obj
1. [file_obj, file_obj2]
1. path
1. [path1, path2]
1. {'filename.txt': file_obj}
1. {'filename.txt', path}
1. [{'filename.txt': file_obj}, {'filenam2.txt', path}]
1. [{'filename.txt': file_obj}, path, file_obj2]
1. sets
1. tuples

Wow, that gets messy. I have no idea what's supported here from reading the docs or even the code itself. I'd guess numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 should work at least. The rest.....?
Add an option to have the watermark image display only once, or to repeat down the page as it currently does.

Even thought the google sheets api v4 supports [batch get](https://developers.google.com/sheets/reference/rest/v4/spreadsheets.values/batchGet) and [batch update](https://developers.google.com/sheets/reference/rest/v4/spreadsheets.values/batchUpdate) operations , it not implemented in pygsheets.

issue_title: add scattering/absorption cross sections and transmission for X-ray
YAML syntax in Fenced code block?
issue_body:
As mentioned in #12 about an enhanced version of csv2table in [pandoc-table-csv-test/panflute-csv2table.ipynb at master · ickc/pandoc-table-csv-test](https://github.com/ickc/pandoc-table-csv-test/blob/master/ipynb/panflute-csv2table.ipynb), I have some questions on the YAML syntax required in the fenced code block.

First, it doesn't allow an opening `---` row, e.g.

```markdown
~~~csv
---
table-width: 1.2
...
**_Fruit_**,~~Price~~,_Number_,`Advantages`
*Bananas~1~*,$1.34,12~units~,"Benefits of eating bananas 
(**Note the appropriately
rendered block markdown**):    

- _built-in wrapper_        
- ~~**bright color**~~

"
*Oranges~2~*,$2.10,5^10^~units~,"Benefits of eating oranges:

- **cures** scurvy
- `tasty`"
~~~
```

will treat the `table-width: 1.2` after the first `---` as the actual table (i.e. not YAML), not the content below.

Second, if there's no YAML like this:

```
~~~csv
**_Fruit_**,~~Price~~,_Number_,`Advantages`
*Bananas~1~*,$1.34,12~units~,"Benefits of eating bananas 
(**Note the appropriately
rendered block markdown**):    

- _built-in wrapper_        
- ~~**bright color**~~

"
*Oranges~2~*,$2.10,5^10^~units~,"Benefits of eating oranges:

- **cures** scurvy
- `tasty`"
~~~
```

It will have an error:

```bash
yaml.scanner.ScannerError: while scanning an alias
  in "<unicode string>", line 1, column 1:
    **_Fruit_**,~~Price~~,_Number_,` ... 
```

i.e. empty YAML still requires a `---`/`...` row to start with.

issue_title: print something while pylint is running
issue_body:
# Issue

CircleCI and probably other services timeout the test if no output is received. It would be helpful to print something while pylint is running to indicate the test hasn't stalled.

# One Idea

Print something during `handle_message`, for example:
```
    def handle_message(self, msg):
        """Get message and append to our data structure"""
        self.data.append(msg)
        print('.', end='')
        sys.stdout.flush()
```
And add another argument to enable this behavior. Downside is it just creates a lot of `.`. Don't know if there's something quick and more clever.

I'm happy to create a PR with this if you like.
this gets picked up by the `Call` check, but some calls produce immutable results!

issue_title: sdist tarball is missing some files and dirs
issue_body:
The tarball uploaded to PyPI via the setup.py sdist command is missing some of the content in the repo because there's no `MANIFEST.in` file. This content includes the `docs` and `examples` subfolders, the `LICENSE` and `Changelog` files and the `requirement-*.txt` files.
Here's the `MANIFEST.in` file that you can add to include them:
```
include *.txt Changelog LICENSE Makefile
include docs/conf.py
recursive-include docs *.rst
graft examples
```
This will make packager's life much easier as the tarball will contain all we need.

In applications that deal with RESTful web APIs I frequently come across situations where the code looks like the folowing.

- API call to get number of "items" available
- Loop over a series of API calls to get those "items" in batches

The problem is that if I use a progress bar to track progress, naïve code suffers from a race condition if the number of "things" available changes between the call to get their count and the calls to fetch them. 

This results in a lot of repetitious boilerplate code along the lines:

    if count <= max_value:
        bar.update(count)

It's easy to forget to include the guard, which results in annoying non-deterministic failures that are hard to catch in testing. 

**It would be nice if the library could (optionally) include logic to ignore updates outside the expected range.**

Using this example from a previous issue:

```python
@pytest.mark.benchmark(group='read')
def test_read(benchmark, method):
    benchmark(read(method=method))

@pytest.mark.benchmark(group='write')
def test_write(benchmark, method):
    benchmark(write(method=method))
```

Let's say this outputs

```
test_read[method0]    264
test_read[method1]    112
test_read[method2]    274
test_read[method3]    130
test_read[method4]    196

test_write[method0]   333
test_write[method1]   99
test_write[method2]   100
test_write[method3]   98
test_write[method4]   79
```

What if you wanted to see how different methods perform relative to a baseline method? Would be cool to be able to enable output like this:

```
test_read[method0][*]   100.0%
test_read[method1]       42.4%
test_read[method2]      103.8%
test_read[method3]       49.2%
test_read[method4]       74.2%

test_write[method0][*]  100.0%
test_write[method1]      29.7%
test_write[method2]      30.0%
test_write[method3]      29.4%
test_write[method4]      23.7%
```

Implementation-wise, maybe `benchmark` could accept a boolean argument `baseline`, which, if set to `True`, would set a benchmark as baseline for the benchmark group, so the code above could be something like

```python
@pytest.mark.benchmark(group='read')
def test_read(benchmark, method):
    benchmark(read(method=method), baseline=(method == 'method0)

@pytest.mark.benchmark(group='write')
def test_write(benchmark, method):
    benchmark(write(method=method), baseline=(method == 'method0'))
```

Perhaps it could be done differently which is not the point, just wanted to share the idea.

issue_title: Change dict's marshaller (PythonDictMarshaller) to inherit from NumpyScalarArrayMarshaller
issue_body:
As of version 0.1.9, the marshaller for ``dict`` types (``hdf5storage.Marshallers.PythonDictMarshaller``) inherits directly from the base marshaller class (``hdf5storage.Marshallers.TypeMarshaller``).

The idea here is to make it inherit from the marshaller for numpy types (``hdf5storage.Marshallers.NumpyScalarArrayMarshaller``) so it can avoid duplicating a lot of code. Converting a ``dict`` to a structured numpy scalar with the dtype for each field being object results in the same structure in the HDF5 other than having the additional Attributes used for numpy types.

Doing this change will make it easier to handle Issue #25 as well as make the handling of null and forward slash characters in key names one place in the code.

The change will change the inheritance structure, which could break any classes inheriting from it.

issue_title: Allow calling external cythonized functions from pythranized functions
issue_body:
Maybe I am really naive, and this is not the way it should be done. In some sense this is related to #461.

## Background
Here we show some attempts to implement a function which performs (`$$\vec{u}. \nabla \vec{u_fft}$$`) through a pseudospectral approach. `u` is a 3D vector in `x, y, z` and `u_fft` is its spectral counterpart in `k_x, k_y, k_z`. The gradient operator `$$\nabla \vec{u}$$`) is calculated as `$$ ifft(1j . \vec{k} (u_fft)) $$`. Where `ifft` means an inverse FFT operation and wavenumber vector `k = (k_x, k_y,k_z)`

## Problem
Enough math. Let's talk code now. Take a look at how we tried to implement this (note: `ifft3d` is a cythonized function)

* Pythran way: https://github.com/fluiddyn/fluidfft/blob/master/fluidfft/fft3d/dream_pythran.py
* Pythran + Cython way: https://github.com/fluiddyn/fluidfft/blob/master/fluidfft/fft3d/dream_cythran.pyx

We would like to implement the rest of the operations using Pythran. We know the exact input and return types of `ifft3d`. However, we have upto 5 different implementations of `ifft3d` depending on which FFT backend is specified / available.

I have tried my best to describe our attempts. If needed I can try to create a simpler test case.

Is it possible for pythran to access a cythonized function?

issue_title: getDevice() is mostly unusable with WinUSB
issue_body:
WinUSB always opens an USB device in exclusive mode. This means that you can't do something like...
```
handle = self.usb_context.openByVendorIDAndProductID(vendor_id, product_id)
serial = handle.getDevice().getSerialNumber()
```
because `USBDevice.getSerialNumber()` opens the device a second time internally and that fails with `LIBUSB_ERROR_ACCESS`.

I think `getDevice()` should return a "pre-opened" `USBDevice` object to avoid this.
…to test under multiple versions of Python. It would have caught the 2.5 and the 2.7 bugs that were just discovered.

Currenly setup.py pins the django version to 1.6.8. We should relax that requirement so we only require the django major version 1.6.x.

We have in our project a few select_for_update() that were blocking each other even though the rows were mutually exclusive.

After a bit of investigation, we found out that the query optimizer will scan the table instead of seeking it by index for small tables. By scanning it, it will lock all the row it scans even if they are not part of the `SELECT` statement.
The way to avoid that is to add 'FORCESEEK' within the `WITH` hint. It has no drawback, but can help concurrent select_for_update with mutually exclusive rows that don't block themself.

Also, after investigation, I think the expected `skip_locked` behavior needs `READPAST` instead of `NOLOCK`.

Proposed version (I will submit a pr):

```
def for_update_sql(self, nowait=False, skip_locked=False):
        """
        Returns the FOR UPDATE SQL clause to lock rows for an update operation.
        """
        if skip_locked:
            return 'WITH (ROWLOCK, UPDLOCK, READPAST, FORCESEEK)'
        elif nowait:
            return 'WITH (NOWAIT, ROWLOCK, UPDLOCK, FORCESEEK)'
        else:
            return 'WITH (ROWLOCK, UPDLOCK, FORCESEEK)'
```

Is there a particular reasons why this shouldn't work with 1.10?

Thank you.

issue_title: Very minor issues: (lack of) compatibility with Micosoft ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server for Linux
issue_body: Re: "Compatible with SQL Server , SQL Server Native Client , Micosoft ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server and FreeTDS ODBC drivers" on the README. I've tested a fair amount of drivers against SQL Server 2008 through 2014 with Django. Since Django 1.7, the Micosoft ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server has been problematic due to multithreading features introduced in Django 1.7. To get runserver to come up, for example, I had to use the following option: ./manage.py runserver --nothreading Additionally, other issues have cropped up, primary with "SQLRowCount returns 0" and SQLParam compatibility. More details here: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/28860521/large-select-query-hangs-using-python-and-unixodbc-connecting-to-sql-server I've switched back to FreeTDS which is working like a charm. We may want to remove the language about supporting the MS ODBC driver on the README unless a solution has been found (I'm happy to issue a PR for the minor change). Thanks for the great work, everyone.

issue_title: Improve object cleanup when connection fails
issue_body:
When wrapping a socket connection in a try, so an exception can be caught if the connection can't be made because the socket server is unreachable, e.g.
try:
    ws = SocketIO('localhost', 8080, wait_for_connection=False)
except:
    handle_exception()

An uncaught exception occurs: 
Exception ignored in: bound method SocketIO.__del__ of <socketIO_client.SocketIO object at 0x7f9be435efd0

This is because errors raised by __del__ output to stderr and are not caught.
The cause of this looks to be in _close() when halt() is called on _heartbeat_thread This is because there is no _heartbeat_thread for the object as an exception occurred during initialization. 

A suggested solution is to wrap the halt() call in a try block, e.g.
        try:
            self._heartbeat_thread.halt()
        except:
            pass

I haven't made a pull request with this as the solution as the author may not want this behavior, so I wanted to check first

`qrcode.tests.test_script.ScriptTest.test_isatty()` and `qrcode.tests.test_script.ScriptTest.test_stdin()` are already configured to be called with `os.isatty()` mocked, however `qrcode.console_scripts.main()` uses `sys.stdout.isatty()`.

Fix:
```
--- qrcode/console_scripts.py
+++ qrcode/console_scripts.py
@@ -5,6 +5,7 @@
 When stdout is a tty the QR Code is printed to the terminal and when stdout is
 a pipe to a file an image is written. The default image format is PNG.
 """
+import os
 import sys
 import optparse
 import qrcode
@@ -52,7 +53,7 @@
     else:
         qr.add_data(data, optimize=opts.optimize)
 
-    if image_factory is None and sys.stdout.isatty():
+    if image_factory is None and os.isatty(sys.stdout.fileno()):
         qr.print_ascii(tty=True)
         return
 
```

issue_title: Tests are skipped if pytest fixtures fail
issue_body: A test marked with `@flaky` will not be marked as failed if the fixture fails for whatever reason - instead, any error is just swallowed and ignored.

The Box API [supports setting passwords on shared links](https://box-content.readme.io/reference#create-a-shared-link-for-a-file) but this feature is as yet missing from the shared link implementation in box-python-sdk ([here](https://github.com/box/box-python-sdk/blob/master/boxsdk/object/item.py#L162)).

I'm currently working up a pull request for this enhancement.

We have 7 workers running on production. We see linear time increase as the number of request per second increases. 
If suppose there are X workers, does that mean only X number of request can be handled at a time and all other requests will be queued which in turn will increase the response time ?

issue_title: "datetime" field type translated as TIMESTAMP SQL Type
issue_body:
Why is this no longer DATETIME?

This shows quite odd behavior in MySQL: the first datetime field receives a default value (current date/time), and an update constraint (again, current datetime), and it is impossible to set it to None (because that just sets it back to the current date).

That breaks a lot of our functionality that operates with datetime fields (all of our tables do) - and it is quite unexpected a change.

Shouldn't the "TIMESTAMP" type be a separate field type if it is desired, and "datetime" be left as it was before?

As pointed out @niphlod on https://github.com/web2py/web2py/issues/667, we can use the better `cursor.lastrowid`.

For more infos: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#lastrowid
Replace all `docs.cloudant.com` links with their equivalent in the Bluemix `console.bluemix.net/docs/` documentation.

issue_title: Writing a customer trainer in MxNet that tracks uncertainty in the weights
issue_body:
I want to implement [Bayes by Backpropagation (BbB)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1505.05424) in MxNet, but two notable features of this algorithm cause me some confusion.

For one, when I look at [example optimizers](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/optimizer.py#L558) I note that they are given the gradient already, so I can specify the weights I want used for that given gradient computation. In BbB, I need to add a noise term to the weights when I compute the gradient. So I would need to control the weights used to compute the gradient.

Second, I need to track both the mean weights and the standard deviation, so I would need to ["attach" more than just the gradient](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/tutorials/gluon/autograd.html).

What are the best approaches for me to solve these problems?

issue_title: Correct way to detect JSON file contains MXNet model
issue_body:
See https://github.com/lutzroeder/Netron/issues/99

`.json` files can contain both Keras models or MXNet models. Would it make sense for one of them to use a different file extension? Should there be a marker in the `.json` to detect it contains MXNet data?

For legacy reasons the GPU related code in mxnet so far has been using c++98 standard, to alleviate the previous problem of nvcc not handling c++11 standard code well. Seems this is no longer the case and c++11 is clearly the future to go. I am opening this issue to propose we move the entire project, including CUDA component to be c++11.

There is nothing we need to do about it, except to turn on the c++11 option for nvcc, as the old code will compile without problem. But this simply eliminates the restriction on not using c++11 features on CUDA code, and hopefully make the project more concise moving forward.

issue_title: Discussion and troubleshooting on PyPI (pip) Nightly Releases
issue_body:
As some of you might already know, the PyPI [mxnet](http://mxnet.io) installation has been available for a while. Recently, we enabled nightly build of the master branch as pip pre-releases. I'm opening this issue to collect feedbacks, suggestions and issue reports for the nightly pre-releases.

Supported variants:
- [mxnet](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet/) on both `OSX` and `Linux`, with CPU-only without MKL support.
- [mxnet-mkl](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-mkl/) on both `OSX` and `Linux`, with CPU-only and with `MKL` support.
- [mxnet-cu75](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu75/) on `Linux`, supports `CUDA-7.5`.
- [mxnet-cu80](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu80/) on `Linux`, supports `CUDA-8.0`.
- [mxnet-cu75mkl](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu75mkl/) on `Linux`, supports `CUDA-7.5` and `MKL`.
- [mxnet-cu80mkl](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu80mkl/) on `Linux`, supports `CUDA-8.0` and `MKL`.

Users can install it via `pip install --pre --upgrade`. Be sure to update `pip` (`setuptools`) to the most recent version, by `pip install --upgrade pip`. 

For bug submissions on issues found in the pre-releases, one can find the corresponding commit hash from the package itself with the following command:
```
$ python -c 'import os, mxnet; print open(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(mxnet.__file__), "COMMIT_HASH")).readline().strip()'
```

class CNNIter(mx.io.DataIter):

        self.provide_data = [('data', (self.batch_size, data.shape[1]))] + init_states
        self.provide_label = [('softmax_label', (self.batch_size,))]

    def __iter__(self):
        print 'iter'
        init_state_names = [x[0] for x in self.init_states]
        for k in range(self.count):
            data = []
            label = []
            seq = random.sample(range(self.data.shape[0]), self.batch_size)
            for i in range(self.batch_size):
                data.append(self.data[seq[i],:])
                label.append(self.label[seq[i]])
            data_all = [mx.nd.array(data)] + self.init_state_arrays
            label_all = [mx.nd.array(label)]
            data_names = ['data'] + init_state_names
            label_names = ['softmax_label']

            data_batch = SimpleBatch(data_names, data_all, label_names, label_all)
            yield data_batch

    def reset(self):
        pass
```

```
def lstm_unroll(num_lstm_layer, seq_len,
                num_hidden, num_label):
    param_cells = []
    last_states = []
    for i in range(num_lstm_layer):
        param_cells.append(LSTMParam(i2h_weight=mx.sym.Variable("l%d_i2h_weight" % i),
                                     i2h_bias=mx.sym.Variable("l%d_i2h_bias" % i),
                                     h2h_weight=mx.sym.Variable("l%d_h2h_weight" % i),
                                     h2h_bias=mx.sym.Variable("l%d_h2h_bias" % i)))
        state = LSTMState(c=mx.sym.Variable("l%d_init_c" % i),
                          h=mx.sym.Variable("l%d_init_h" % i))
        last_states.append(state)
    assert(len(last_states) == num_lstm_layer)

    # embeding layer
    data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
    label = mx.sym.Variable('softmax_label')
    wordvec = mx.sym.SliceChannel(data=data, num_outputs=seq_len, squeeze_axis=1)

    hidden_all = []
    for seqidx in range(seq_len):
        hidden = wordvec[seqidx]
        for i in range(num_lstm_layer):
            next_state = lstm(num_hidden, indata=hidden,
                              prev_state=last_states[i],
                              param=param_cells[i],
                              seqidx=seqidx, layeridx=i)
            hidden = next_state.h
            last_states[i] = next_state
        hidden_all.append(hidden)

    hidden_concat = mx.sym.Concat(*hidden_all, dim=0)
    pred = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=hidden_concat, num_hidden=num_label)

    label = mx.sym.Reshape(data=label, shape=(-1,))
    label = mx.sym.Cast(data = label, dtype = 'int32')
    sm = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(data=pred, label = label, name='softmax')

    return sm

```

model.fit(X = train)
```

## Minimum reproducible example
```
import mxnet as mx
import os
import subprocess
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import tarfile

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=DeprecationWarning)

class SimpleIter(mx.io.DataIter):
    def __init__(self, data_names, data_shapes, data_gen,
                 label_names, label_shapes, label_gen, num_batches=10):
        self._provide_data = zip(data_names, data_shapes)
        self._provide_label = zip(label_names, label_shapes)
        self.num_batches = num_batches
        self.data_gen = data_gen
        self.label_gen = label_gen
        self.cur_batch = 0

    def __iter__(self):
        return self

    def reset(self):
        self.cur_batch = 0

    def __next__(self):

    @property
    def provide_label(self):
        return self._provide_label

    def next(self):
        if self.cur_batch < self.num_batches:
            self.cur_batch += 1
            data = [mx.nd.array(g(d[1])) for d,g in zip(self._provide_data, self.data_gen)]
            label = [mx.nd.array(g(d[1])) for d,g in zip(self._provide_label, self.label_gen)]
            return mx.io.DataBatch(data, label)
        else:
            raise StopIteration

num_classes = 10

net = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=net, name='fc1', num_hidden=64)
net = mx.sym.Activation(data=net, name='relu1', act_type="relu")
net = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=net, name='fc2', num_hidden=num_classes)
net = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(data=net, name='softmax')
print(net.list_arguments())
print(net.list_outputs())

n = 32
data_iter = SimpleIter(['data'], [(n, 100)],
                  [lambda s: np.random.uniform(-1, 1, s)],
                  ['softmax_label'], [(n,)],
                  [lambda s: np.random.randint(0, num_classes, s)])

mod = mx.mod.Module(symbol=net)
mod.fit(data_iter, num_epoch=5)
```
Actually this code is same as the official mxnet tutorial. ( https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/tutorials/basic/data.html )

## What have you tried to solve it?
It seems `def _parse_data_desc(data_names, label_names, data_shapes, label_shapes)` and `def _check_names_match(data_names, data_shapes, name, throw)` have bugs.

issue_title: Use of AdminMediaHandler causes runserver_plus/runserver inconsistencies in Django 1.4.x
issue_body:
When calling ``python manage.py runserver_plus --nostatic`` in Django 1.4.3, AdminMediaHandler is used (at ``runserver_plus.py`` line 146) to serve admin static files, using a hardcoded path.  

This does not make sense with Django 1.4 and django.contrib.staticfiles, which is smart enough to find the version of the admin static files that you are intending to use.  (For example, if using grappelli).  ``python manage.py runserver --nostatic`` and ``python manage.py collectstatic``  traverse your static files to serve the appropriate (highest priority) file.  This is the expected behavior.

A workaround of course is to specify ``--adminmedia`` as you did before Django 1.4.  However this is not guaranteed to maintain consistency with ``collectstatic``, and it would be nice to know production and development were the same.

I'm happy to submit a patch but I'm not sure what it's doing there in the first place (presumably for some versions of Django it is needed?)

issue_title: '$.attr' returns undefined for boolean inputs even if they are in attribute list
issue_body:
This is pretty minor, but it's an actual bug in production for us, and I couldn't find any issues that seemed to cover this.

##### Overview

jQuery migrate causes `$.attr` to return undefined for boolean attributes if the requested boolean's property is false, even if the attribute is not. I am experiencing this problem because Firefox is incorrectly giving a checkbox input a property of `checked: false` on a live site. This is causing jQuery migrate to make `$el.attr('checked')` return `false`, although it should be returning `"checked"` because "checked=checked" is still in the attributes list.

##### Versions

This is using jQuery version 1.11.2 and jQuery migrate 1.2.1, although I believe the versions are irrelevant.

##### Simple Reproduction

Make a checkbox element that is checked, then set it's checked property to false. Then try to get its checked attribute and it will return undefined.
```
var $input = $('<input id="shippingIsBilling" name="shippingIsBilling" checked="checked" class="js-shippingIsBilling" type="checkbox">').appendTo('body')
$input.get(0).checked = false;
$input.get(0).outerHTML
// "<input id="shippingIsBilling" name="shippingIsBilling" checked="checked" class="js-shippingIsBilling" type="checkbox">"
$input.attr('checked')
// undefined
$input.prop('checked')
// false
```

##### Why it's a bug

jQuery without the migrate plugin returns "checked" for `$input.attr('checked')`. However, the logic for the migrate plugin forces boolean attributes to agree with `$.prop` in order to return true - https://github.com/jquery/jquery-migrate/blob/master/src/attributes.js#L46

##### Proposed Solution

I think that if an item is in the attributes list, it should probably return that from a `$.attr` call, instead of short-circuiting to a ( in this case, incorrect ) call to `prop`.

##### Live Reproduction

I'm only actually experiencing this problem in Firefox ( I'm on version 40.0.3, but also experienced it in 40.0.2 ). It works as expected in Chrome 44.0.2403.157.

This bug should be able to be recreated on https://www.smartdestinations.com/checkout/sdi-checkout.ep . You'll need to [add an item to your cart](http://www.smartdestinations.com/pass.ep?filters=_d_Bos_Prod_Go) first. Then, go to checkout but don't do anything. You should see 3 delivery options. Refresh the page. If all went right ( wrong? ), you should now see 5 delivery options, and can explore the bug.

You can now run the following snippets in the console:

```
$(".js-shippingIsBilling").get(0).outerHTML
// "<input id="shippingIsBilling" name="shippingIsBilling" checked="checked" class="js-shippingIsBilling" type="checkbox">"
$(".js-shippingIsBilling").attr('checked')
// undefined
$(".js-shippingIsBilling").prop('checked')
// false
```

jQuery without the migrate plugin returns "checked" for `$(".js-shippingIsBilling").attr('checked')`. I tested locally that this is true - there's not a public version of the page without the migrate plugin.

I believe this is some kind of bug in Firefox caching input values, although the input is never being set to false, so that doesn't REALLY make sense.

I think Firefox should stop doing whatever it's doing that's messing this up, and if it did, I wouldn't care about this "bug" being fixed in jQuery migrate. I'm not sure how to describe it any more clearly than this report, so I'll probably just link this in bugzilla, if it will let me.
In the theme mapper, the user should be able to switch the text editor to fullscreen (just like the inspectors already do).
