issue_title: Will pyzmq fail in an MPI scenario?
issue_body:
HI all,
I was using pyzmq and mpi to build a multi-node(machine)/ multi-gpu deep learning project.
The code runs well in a single-node, multi-gpu scenario, while fails in the multi-node one.

It fails when the following `query` happens:
```
# definition
self.ccontext = zmq.Context()
self.csocket.bind('tcp://*:{}'.format(port)) 

# try
try:
    query = self.csocket.recv_json(flags=zmq.NOBLOCK)
except zmq.Again:  # if a query has not happened, try again
    continue
except zmq.ZMQError as exc:
    raise PlatoonError("while receiving using ZMQ socket", exc)

```

It keeps raising zmq.Again multiple time, and then fails (timeout). I'm not sure whether it is a pyzmq problem or something I mis-used (like using ipc instead of tcp?). Any advice is helpful.

Thank you

I apology in advance if question is lame as I'm not familiar with manylinux and cython to the bone.

Currently pyzmq wheels for Linux are shipped with pre-built zeromq. libzmq is available for the popular repositories either from the mainstream or from custom repositories. To my understanding the convenience of manylinux is to ship only essential binaries and allow the linker to resolve dependencies. That allows system administrator to independently update libraries.

In my case pyzmq, even 17.0.0b1, is still shipped with libzmq 4.1.6 while I'd like to use 4.2.2 (no draft) due to important bug fixes. My only options are to either build my own wheels or install everything that's for a manual build right where I deploy.

issue_title: zmq.auth.Authenticator should set public key so that it is gettable as frame property
issue_body:
zmq.auth.Authenticator should set public key used by a specific client (or as the very minimum, the fingerprint of the public key) so that it is obtainable via zmq.Frame's get() function as a property for each message to identify the respective client. Since configure_curve() just takes an entire folder with potentially a lot of keys to allow different clients/accounts, this is required to be able to identify _which_ client specifically has been allowed to connect and is currently speaking.

If such functionality is already available, please add a mention of the property's name in the documentation: http://pyzmq.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/zmq.auth.html

issue_title: Suggestion: Warn for non-string keys or values in kwargs in <class>.from_str_params()
issue_body:
I figured that when using the class method [from_str_params()](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/task.py#L296-L310), if accidentally submitting values that are not strings, one can get hard to debug errors.

E.g. with the following snippet, where the zero in the dictionary is an int rather than a string:

``` python
import luigi

class ATask(luigi.Task):
    avalue = luigi.IntParameter()
    def output(self):
        return 'afile.txt'
    def run(self):
        with self.output().open('w') as outf:
            outf.write(str(self.avalue))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    t = ATask.from_str_params({'avalue': 0})
```

... I get the following error:

``` bash
$ python error.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "error.py", line 12, in <module>
    t = ATask.from_str_params({'avalue': 0})
  File "/home/samuel/.pyenv/versions/2.7.8/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/task.py", line 297, in from_str_params
    value = param.parse_from_input(param_name, params_str[param_name])
  File "/home/samuel/.pyenv/versions/2.7.8/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/parameter.py", line 296, in parse_from_input
    (param_name, "--" + param_name.replace('_', '-')))
luigi.parameter.MissingParameterException: No value for 'avalue' (--avalue) submitted and no default value has been assigned.
```

This specific error, I figured was due to the [line "if not x" in line 291 of parameter.py](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/parameter.py#L291), which results in a "no" in this if statement, due to the integer zero value being sent.

Maybe one could add checks that all the keys and values really are string, in from_str_params()?

Currently, if we want to work with some pull request with GitPython, it is not possible afaik to allow unrelated histories.

> The default behavior has changed since git 2.9:
> 
>     "git merge" used to allow merging two branches that have no common base by default, which led to a brand new history of an existing project created and then get pulled by an unsuspecting maintainer, which allowed an unnecessary parallel history merged into the existing project. The command has been taught not to allow this by default, with an escape hatch "--allow-unrelated-histories" option to be used in a rare event that merges histories of two projects that started their lives independently.
> 
> See the git release [changelog](https://github.com/git/git/blob/master/Documentation/RelNotes/2.9.0.txt#L58-L68) for more information.

I can edit sidebar/other placeholder contents on the main page of the blog as expected. However, when I try to do the same from, for example, a post view, I get the following error:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1115667/14950265/bc5a0974-1050-11e6-94b3-6bfdabfce40f.png)

Is there something odd with my setup or is this expected behaviour? If it's the latter, could the error message be amended? I suspect this will be quite confusing for our users.

issue_title: Boostrapping fails with AttributeError: module 'html.parser' has no attribute 'HTMLParseError'
issue_body:
On my MAC OS X bootstrapping fails with the following.

AttributeError: module 'html.parser' has no attribute 'HTMLParseError'
Failure occurred. Do you want to cleanup by removing /Users/ghachey/Development/Python/tutorial-project? [Y/N] Y

I have Python 3.5 installed using brew. I use Python's venv for virtual environment. This was done in a virgin virtual environment. I selected most default from the script (stablet djangocms and stablet Django). It seeems the script choose to install Django 1.7 which had a known bug using HTMLParseError which is deprecated from Python 3.3 onwards and removed in Python 3.5, as per the [official documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/html.parser.html#html.parser.HTMLParseError)

I'm new to djangocms so I may have done something wrong though I am not new to Python and Django.

Firstly thank you Kennith for Pipenv and being able to meet you and the rest of the pipenv team at PyCon this year. Trying to now use pipenv in our daily workflow at work and have come into a few snags.

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
At work all our systems are dockerised and both local development and deployments / production use the same docker image. We `docker run --rm <image> bash` and do our development from there. Our Dockerfile contains these build steps
```
RUN pip install pipenv
COPY Pipfile Pipfile.lock /tmp/
RUN cd /tmp && pipenv install --deploy --system --dev
...
```
The issue comes when we start developing inside the image and want to install new packages. As its a container we dont need any virtualenvs. There seems to be no way to install a single package straight to system. This has caused a bit of confusion as all the base packages are importable (since they where installed from a Pipfile with --system, but a new package installed while developing with `pipenv install x` is not importable (from system python) as it goes into a virtualenv.

This leads us to having to install these packages twice. `pipenv install x` (to get it into the Pipfile) and then `pipenv install --system` to then reinstall the pipefile with the new package for system python to be able to see it.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
It would be nice to have 1) --system when installing of specific packages and then 2) a way of setting --system globally so we can set it as an ENV in our dockerfile as pipenv in docker would only ever be used against system. I feel this would be the use case for most people doing development inside docker.

I have looked into the code and noticed there are some undocumented envs that would turn on --system by default (but its not 100% clear and not a pipenv specific variable, seems like I need to `export VIRTUAL_ENV= `so python is able to cast it to False correctly https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/environments.py#L52). But as a found out it does not apply for installing specific packages. Just from the pipfile.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
na

issue_title: Dev package overwrites version in package
issue_body:
##### Describe your environment

1. MacOS
1. Python version: `Python 3.6.3`
1. Pipenv version: `pipenv, version 9.0.0`

##### Expected result

`dev-packages` do not override `packages`

##### Actual result

Locking [packages] dependencies…
Updated Pipfile.lock (548cfe)!

issue_title: dateparser should pin all requirements versions
issue_body:
[requiremenst.txt](https://github.com/scrapinghub/dateparser/blob/master/requirements.txt) file has dependencies names without versions specified. This is not good, if some dependency will do breaking update at some point dateparser will break. All packages should probably be pinned to minimal versions that work ok. 

http://nvie.com/posts/pin-your-packages/

I'm talking about this because today I woke up and saw following warnings in logs in production:

```
The default 'Loader' for 'load(stream)' without further arguments can be unsafe.
Use 'load(stream, Loader=ruamel.yaml.Loader)' explicitly if that is OK.
Alternatively include the following in your code:

  import warnings
  warnings.simplefilter('ignore', ruamel.yaml.error.UnsafeLoaderWarning)

In most other cases you should consider using 'safe_load(stream)'
  cls._yaml_data = load_yaml(data).pop('settings', {})
/home/pawel/.virtualenvs/scrapy/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dateparser/languages/loader.py:46: UnsafeLoaderWarning: 
The default 'Loader' for 'load(stream)' without further arguments can be unsafe.
Use 'load(stream, Loader=ruamel.yaml.Loader)' explicitly if that is OK.
Alternatively include the following in your code:

  import warnings
  warnings.simplefilter('ignore', ruamel.yaml.error.UnsafeLoaderWarning)

```

what happened is that dateparser relies on ruamel.yaml, yaml updated package and added this deprecation warning, I see this is (probably) fixed in dateparser, but not released yet. It's just a warning so no big deal, BUT it is just as likely that at some point yaml will do breaking update, and I'll get broken project because of dateparser not specyfing version numbers.

Name: dateparser
Version: 0.6.0

`
In [1]: import dateparser

In [2]: dateparser.parse('2018-03-21T00:36:04-00:00')

In [3]:
`
if strip '-00:00' works.

There’s a relatively new standard [primitive data type][] in JavaScript: the [symbol][]. Since JavaScript engines have added support for symbols several years ago, and since they are part of the standard ECMAScript specification, their use has been progressively increasing.

JS-YAML should support `dump`ing and `load`ing data containing symbols. They would probably look like `!!js/symbol description` and `!!js/symbolFor globalKey`. They would also probably be safe for `DEFAULT_SAFE_SCHEMA`.

I ran into this lack of support myself when using [Node-Tap](https://github.com/tapjs/node-tap): `t.strictEqual(Symbol(''), Symbol(''))` causes an `unacceptable kind of an object to dump [object Symbol]` error to occur in `writeNode` (`node_modules/js-yaml/lib/js-yaml/dumper.js:738:13`).



[primitive data type]: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Glossary/Primitive
[symbol]: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Glossary/symbol

Today we can choose "poll_interval", "max_proc" and "max_proc_per_cpu". "max_proc" and "max_proc_per_cpu" are for limiting a maximum of jobs that can be running.

If "poll_interval" is high that maximum would never be reached. That happens because after each poll interval we start only one job.

E.g.: poll_interval = 30 and some spider takes about 2 minutes to finish. The maxium jobs that would run in this case is 4~5, no matter what "max_proc" and "max_proc_per_cpu" values are.

Maybe it should have an option "jobs_to_poll". With that we could choose how many jobs to poll each time, instead of only one.

What you guys think about that?

Thank you

issue_title: EllipsePixelRegion Angle argument is in radians, not degrees
issue_body:
When defining an EllipsePixelRegion, the angle argument is specified in degrees, but performs rotations as if it is in radians. 

For example:
`EllipsePixelRegion(center, major_axis, minor_axis, angle=90*u.deg` returns an ellipse with a rotation angle of 90 _radians_.
`EllipsePixelRegion(center, major_axis, minor_axis, angle=1.57*u.deg` returns an ellipse with a rotation angle of 90 degrees (1.57 radians).

1. should include fwoker.query in lpad query request?

here is my steps:

I add `test_category` to `my_fworker.yaml` , like:

```yaml
name: SJTU_Pi
category: test_category
query: '{}'
env:
   <other settings>

and my FWorker.query:

```python
{'$or': [{'spec._fworker': {'$exists': False}},

submit a workflow with category

from pymatgen.ext.matproj import MPRester

issue_title: Problem Compiling NuPIC on Mac OS 10.10.2
issue_body:
NuPIC 0.2.0 (binary) and 0.2.1.dev0 (git checkout) will not currently install/build on a Mac. The same error results using both approaches.

> What did you do? (steps to reproduce)

Setup dependencies:

```
# First run XCode and opt to "Install Optional Components"
$ xcode-select --install # Also needed
$ sudo easy_install pip
$ sudo pip install wheel
$ sudo pip install numpy
```

Steps to install the binary:

```
$ sudo pip install nupic
```

Steps to compile:

```
$ export NUPIC=/Users/dwood/code/nupic
$ mkdir $NUPIC; cd !$
$ git clone https://github.com/numenta/nupic.git # or clone via GitHub Desktop
$ ARCHFLAGS="-arch x86_64" python setup.py install --user
```

> What happened? (actual results)

The same error occurs in both cases:

```
/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/include/python2.7/object.h:116:56: note: expanded from macro 'Py_SIZE'
#define Py_SIZE(ob)             (((PyVarObject*)(ob))->ob_size)
                                ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^~~~~~~
_imagingft.c:237:34: error: use of undeclared identifier 'FT_GLYPH_BBOX_SUBPIXELS'
...
error: command 'cc' failed with exit status 1
```

> What should have happened? (expected results)

The binary should have installed, and the code should have compiled without error.

> Additional Information (operating system, python version, NuPIC commit SHA, etc.)

> Mac OS X 10.10.2
> Python 2.7.6 (Apple-provided system python)
> NuPIC binary release 0.2.0 and GitHub source 0.2.1.dev0.

Depends on #136, #137, #138, #139.

issue_title: Error in authorization if no root factory is defined
issue_body:
```
  File "/home/mathieu/Code/Mozilla/readinglist/.venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyramid/config/views.py", line 241, in _permitted
    permission)
  File "/home/mathieu/Code/Mozilla/cliquet/cliquet/authorization.py", line 27, in permits
    if context.prefixed_userid:
AttributeError: DefaultRootFactory instance has no attribute 'prefixed_userid'
```

If the permission system is not used (e.g. readinglist), then it's a pity to query the permission backend. 

Suggestions:
* Add default value for root factory kwarg to ``cliquet.authorization.RouteFactory`` to ``@register`` decorator
* Document clearly that ``cliquet.articles_read_principals = system.Authenticated`` can be used to bypass the permissions backend
* Or otherwise, add a simple fallback like this one in authorization policy:

```python
    def permits(self, context, principals, permission):
        # If no root factory, just required authentication.
        if not isinstance(context, RouteFactory):
            return Authenticated in principals
```

We should have a way to define the service URL / scheme so it's set explicitely.

ref. https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/pull/151

issue_title: Document how to serve static content
issue_body:
The Dockerflow requires the app to serve static content on its own.

For Django the best option currently is to use [Whitenoise](https://whitenoise.readthedocs.io/) as it does this:

> WhiteNoise takes care of best-practices for you, for instance:
>
>    Serving compressed content (gzip and Brotli formats, handling Accept-Encoding and Vary headers correctly)
>    Setting far-future cache headers on content which won’t change

Additionally it supports non-Django pure-WSGI use as well, so should work for Flask and Pyramid, too.

What license applies to the bundled version of Highcharts? Highcharts has been non-free software for quite a while and requires a license for non-commercial use. Unfortunately, the link in the highcharts.js file (www.highcharts.com/license) is dead.

Originally by *fasouto* on 2010-08-20

People with limited mobility or visually impaired need to navigate with keyboard shortcuts. The implementation it's really easy, adding the "accesskey=[char]" to the HTML.

There's already an access key in the CERN library website to go to the main content(try pressing Shift+Alt+1 on Firefox) but I can't find it in my Invenio installation.

There's a de facto standard promoted by the UK goverment with the following keyboard shortcuts:

S - Skip navigation
1 - Home page
2 - What's new
3 - Site map
4 - Search
5 - Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
6 - Help
7 - Complaints procedure
8 - Terms and conditions
9 - Feedback form
0 - Access key details

This is possible by actually providing the user not the real URL but an intermediate Invenio URL that will then take care of logging the click and redirecting to the real external URL.

issue_title: API to access the status of a transaction
issue_body:
Currently there is `isDoomed()` to check whether a transaction is doomed.
But there are no methods to check the other status (like `Active` or `Commit failed`).
To check for these status it is currently necessary to do something like this:

```
from transaction._transaction import Status
if transaction.get().status == Status.ACTIVE:
    pass
```

It would be nice to 
* either have methods on the transaction for the status checks
* or a way to access the Status constants without importing from `_transaction` (which feels like using internals instead of the API).

What do you think?
We would need the fix for Plone 5.1. Thanks in advance!

This would make it compatible with the `IConnection` adapter provided by `zope.keyreference` (assuming the object it annotates is persistent), which could simplify some use cases.

For pickle compatibility this could be a property that reads `self.obj`.

Are there any downsides?

When using filter widget and a countable checkbox widget, description in filter widget contains html tags (as text). For instance (sorry for the italian text): [X]Categoria [X]Foto Storica (40708) [X] Remove all

Imported from https://projects.linaro.org/browse/QA-1744
right now the metadata items will be more or less displayed in random order, based on the sorting in python dictionaries. we should probably pre-sort those and return a list of tuples (think .items()). however we need to take into account that custom templates might be assuming metadata is a dict so there will be a API break there.

issue_title: No instructions for utilizing buildozer in Arch linux (i686 / x86_64)
issue_body:
**NOTE:** The following commands assume that your user has sudo access. If not, this can be accomplished by "usermod -G wheel {user}" without quotes, as root. It is also necessary to uncomment the following line in /etc/sudoers:
%wheel ALL=(ALL) ALL


**If platform is Arch x86_64 Enable multilib repository**
=========================================
Uncomment the following in /etc/pacman.conf:
[multilib]
Include = /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist

**Update the package database** 
=======================
sudo pacman -Syu

**If platform is Arch x86_64:**
====================
sudo pacman -S jdk7-openjdk \
python2 \
python2-pip \
python2-kivy \
mesa-libgl \
lib32-mesa-libgl \
lib32-sdl2 \
lib32-sdl2_image \
lib32-sdl2_mixer \
sdl2_ttf \
unzip \
gcc-multilib \
gcc-libs-multilib

If prompted with the following, answer 'y' for both prompts:
::gcc-multilib and gcc are in conflict. Remove gcc?
::gcc-libs-multilib and gcc-libs are in conflict. Remove gcc-libs?

**If platform is Arch x86:**
==================
sudo pacman -S jdk7-openjdk \
python2 \
python2-pip \
python2-kivy \
mesa-libgl \
sdl2 \
sdl2_image \
sdl2_mixer \
sdl2_ttf \
unzip


**Install Cython:**
===========
sudo pip2 install cython


**Install buildozer:**
==============
git clone http://github.com/kivy/buildozer
sudo python2 setup.py install

**DONE!**

From here you should be able to run the buildozer build process.
- buildozer init
- modify the buildozer.conf as necessary
- buildozer android debug serve

`import cStringsIO ` fails with Python3 
According to "What's new in Python 3" 

> The StringIO and cStringIO modules are gone. Instead, import the io module and use io.StringIO or io.BytesIO for text and data respectively.

Also there are problems with `bytes` and `str` conversions.

I'd like a way to make my CLI exit with a custom, non-zero code when using Python-fire.

One option might be to have a specific value you could return from your Fire function in order to exit with a particular code. This could be accomplished with, say, a simple ExitCode class wrapping an integer.

Possibly a better idea is to have a special exception class, e.g. ExitCodeError, such that raising an instance of a that class (or a subclass thereof), modifies the exit code of the CLI.

issue_title: Problems installing Python bindings on Mac
issue_body:
Hello! I'm having some trouble installing the Python bindings on Mac OS, and just thought I'd mention it here in case anyone had similar trouble. This is within an Anaconda environment, Python 3.

Move into directory and install:

    $ cd /path/to/sentencepiece
    $ ./autogen.sh
    $ ./configure
    $ make
    $ make install

Success. Try to install Python bindings:

    $ python setup.py build
    Package sentencepiece was not found in the pkg-config search path.
    Perhaps you should add the directory containing `sentencepiece.pc'
    to the PKG_CONFIG_PATH environment variable

    Failed to find sentencepiece pkgconfig

Fix the path.

And try again:

    $ python setup.py build
    running build
    running build_py
    running build_ext
    building '_sentencepiece' extension
    gcc -Wno-unused-result -Wsign-compare -Wunreachable-code -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -I/anaconda3/envs/python3/include -arch x86_64 -I/anaconda3/envs/python3/include -arch x86_64 -I/anaconda3/envs/python3/include/python3.6m -c sentencepiece_wrap.cxx -o build/temp.macosx-10.7-x86_64-3.6/sentencepiece_wrap.o -std=c++11 -g -O2 -I/Users/neubig/usr/include

The strange thing is that when I run the command on its own by copy-pasting things seem to work.

issue_title: Define and implement a versioning pattern on the web API
issue_body:
Currently we serve : 

- `api.openfisca.fr`, the "legacy" web API. 
- `fr.openfisca.org/api/v2`, the "preview" web API.

A breaking change in the france tax benefit system (e.g. variable renaming) or in the OpenFisca behaviour is _not_ reflected in the URL.

Ideally, a breaking change in the API should imply an URL change. 
We thus need to agree on a URL pattern.

## Open questions:
 - Should the `openfisca-france` major version be explicited in the URL ?
 - Should the `openfisca-core` major version be explicited in the URL ?
  
## Possibilities:
- `fr.openfisca.org/api/v5/parameters`, where `v5` implies a specific major version for france **and** for the API behaviour.
- `fr.openfisca.org/api/v5/parameters`, where `v5` implies a specific major version **just** for the API behaviour.
- `fr.openfisca.org/api/v2/fr-19/parameters`, where `fr-19` explicits the `france` version, and `v2` explicits the API behaviour.
- `fr.openfisca.org/api/of-18/fr-19`, where `fr-19` explicits the `france` version, and `of-18` explicits the core version.
- `fr.openfisca.org/api/v2/` with no guarantee at the moment (status quo)

## Possible strategies:
- Massive renaming in france to avoid later breaking changes
- Major release every _n_ months
- Meta-API to serve several api versions (costly)

## Deliverable:

The preview API is served on the chosen URL.

J'ai peur qu'il y ait un problème dans une variable appelée pour calculer presque toutes les cotisations sociales : [assiette_cotisations_sociales_prive](http://legislation.openfisca.fr/variables/assiette_cotisations_sociales_prive)
L'assiette est à 1457€ mensuels pour les bas revenus (< 500€/mois), ce qui conduit à des cotisations sociales forfaitaires de 206€ mensuels pour ces bas revenus, d'où un salaire_imposable négatif pour les revenus < 200€/mois.
Or, la formule de cette variable semble douteuse : 
`max_(assiette, smic_proratise * not_(apprenti)) * (assiette > 0)`
En gros, pour les bas revenus, le smic_proratise est appliqué comme assiette, d'où le montant forfaitaire. La condition "assiette > 0" est peut-être à revoir : ne serait-ce pas "assiette > xxx €" ?
Je laisse cette énigme à qui voudra bien se plonger dans les textes de loi.

This is just a suggestion. I feel that the credentials handling code is a bit cumbersome to use, with a lot of little functions for saving each setting. Although this works, it will become a mess if we add more settings.

My suggestion:
- Credentials class
- Pass the configuration file in the constructor (if not set, have some default configuration path)
- One attribute per setting (`api_key`, `base_url`)
- One method to `load` and a second one to `save` configuration. Probably calling `load` in the constructor, and `save` in the attribute setters.
- Allow passing a `Credentials` instance to the CF context. This way, all settings are encapsulated with a single parameter.

This is my very rough idea. I think it's worth it for future extensibility as more settings may very well be needed. What do you think? @andy-esch @stuartlynn

issue_title: Missing access Token parameter with OAuth2Session, but plain ol' requests works.
issue_body:
I'm building an api integration, and I can get it working using plain requests yet not with requests-oauthlib. The traceback goes to the oauthlib itself, but this is similar to unsolved issue 286 on this repo, so I'll post here for now. 

The api I'm hitting isn't public, so I'll do my best to provide context even if you can't directly reproduce. 

Here's what works with requests. This is at the callback stage, and the state is definitely the same state from the authorization url:
```
access_url = access_token_url + '?grant_type=authorization_code' + '&code=' + request.GET.get('code', '') + '&client_id=' + my_client_id + '&redirect_uri=' + redirect_uri + '&client_secret=' + client_secret + '&state=' + request.session['oauth_state']
token = requests.post(access_url)
```

Here's what fails with requests-oauthlib:

```
myobject = OAuth2Session(client_id = client_id, redirect_uri = redirect_uri, state = request.session['oauth_state'])
token = myobject.fetch_token(access_token_url, authorization_response=request.build_absolute_uri(), client_secret=client_secret
```

I'm fairly sure that the request.build_absolute_uri() is not the problem, because that part works for other API integrations; that most certainly returns the full url it needs to parse. 

Anyways here's the error traceback:
```

File "/app/.heroku/python/lib/python3.6/site-packages/requests_oauthlib/oauth2_session.py" in fetch_token
  244.         self._client.parse_request_body_response(r.text, scope=self.scope)

File "/app/.heroku/python/lib/python3.6/site-packages/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/clients/base.py" in parse_request_body_response
  411.         self.token = parse_token_response(body, scope=scope)

File "/app/.heroku/python/lib/python3.6/site-packages/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/parameters.py" in parse_token_response
  379.     validate_token_parameters(params)

File "/app/.heroku/python/lib/python3.6/site-packages/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/parameters.py" in validate_token_parameters
  389.         raise MissingTokenError(description="Missing access token parameter.")
```

So, somehow oauthlib raises an error because it can't find the access token parameter when it tries to validate whether parse_token_response() worked. So something seems to be going wrong at parse_token_response().

And this is what the token looks like when we do obtain the token:

```
{
"access_token": “<access_token>”,
"expires_in": 36000.0,
"refresh_token”: “<refresh_token>”
}
```

If someone can tell me how to inspect exactly what raw http requests are being sent by object.fetch_token(), that would also help me diagnose further. Is there a way to inspect the oauth2session object to find that, or does anyone happen to know an easy way to find that for a django app on heroku? (it's not in heroku logs)

Thanks for contributing to such an elegant package. The overall quality really makes me want to fix this rather than use the plain old requests code in my app.

issue_title: Client_id and client_secret get duplicated in fetch_token()
issue_body:
After #229, passing `client_id` & `client_secret` in `fetch_token()` duplicates them into both request body and as basic auth headers, causing following error at least on PingFederate (a commercial OAuth2 server):

    oauthlib.oauth2.rfc6749.errors.InvalidClientError: (invalid_client) The client MUST NOT use more than one authentication method in each request.

Shouldn't the library omit the fields from request body, if they are injected as HTTP Basic auth header?

issue_title: Allow easy paging for list operations
issue_body:
Taking the comment on https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/pull/889/files#r31042158 over here.

Requiring explicit paging for topics is really ugly, there must be a better way than:

```python
all_topics = []

topics, token = client.list_topics()
all_topics.extend(topics)
while token is not None:
  topics, token = client.list_topics(page_token=token)
  all_topics.extend(topics)
```

If our concern is "people could make a boatload of requests and they don't realize it", we could always limit this like we do with the recursive deleting in storage?

```python
topics = client.list_topics(limit=100)
```

We can do a bunch of this with the `page_size` parameter, but that might mean that I have to wait for *everything* to come back before starting any work, which seems kind of ridiculous.

It'd be really nice if that limit and page-size stuff was both there, so it's easy to do things like "I want the first 5000 topics, and I want to pull them from the server in chunks of 50":

```python
for topic in client.list_topics(page_size=50, limit=5000):
  push_work_to_other_system(topic)
```

---

To add a bit more context, I'd like to toss out: what if we made all of our list operations return iterators?

The use cases I see here are...

1. I want everything, give it all to me (`for topic in list_topics()`)
1. I want up to N things, stop giving them to me then (`for topic in list_topics(limit=100)`)
1. I don't know how many I want, I'll know when I want to stop though... (`for topics in list_topics(): if topic.name == 'foo': break`)
1. Combination of the previous two (I don't know when I want to stop, but don't let me go on forever, kill it at say... 1000)
1. I want to pick up where I left off, I saved a token somewhere (sort of like `offset`)! (`for topic in list_topics(page_token=token, limit=100)`)

The "let's just always return `page, page_token`" thing doesn't really make all of those use-cases all that fun... But if we always return iterators, they are all easy.

Further, let's say I have a weird case where I just want one page worth of stuff... `list_topics().get_current_page()` could return what you want, no?

I'm able to deploy it but I keep getting this error:
"DistributionNotFound: The 'gcloud' distribution was not found and is required by the application"
I would like to see some documentation around the flags that can be passed instead of trying to follow through all the pieces of code where this parameter is passed.

issue_title: Pub/Sub: subscriber waiting on future.result() blocks forever
issue_body:
when pubsub endpoints changed recently, it broke our code (after working fine for about a year).  we have ported our code to the new async API in 0.29.2 to get it working again.

following the documentation, we're calling `.open()` to register a callback; when run, it stores the message locally, then calls `message.ack()`.  in the main loop, we're waiting on the Future's `.result()` to be returned so we can do some bottom-half processing on the stored message.  however, our subscriber blocks forever waiting for the Future's .result() method to return, and I don't think this is how it's supposed to work based on the documentation:

> The `pubsub_v1.subscriber.policy.thread.Policy.open` method returns a `pubsub_v1.subscriber.futures.Future`, which is both the interface to wait on messages (e.g. block the primary thread) and to address exceptions.
> 
> To block the thread you are in while messages are coming in the stream, use the `.pubsub_v1.subscriber.futures.Future.result` method [...] You can also use this for error handling; any exceptions that crop up on a thread will be set on the future.

if it's "the interface to wait on messages," we're quite puzzled, because it never returns any results; it seems to block forever.  note that the same call in the publisher *does not* block; it returns the published message ID right away, which is what we expect.  only the subscriber has this strange behavior of never returning the result.

distilled to simple test case, here's the publisher:
```python
topic_path = 'projects/ourproject/topics/test12345'
publisher = pubsub.PublisherClient()
publisher.publish(topic_path, b'New Message {0}'.format(datetime.now()))
```

and the subscriber (run this first to create the topic):
```python
def callback(message):
    print 'Callback triggered'
    print message
    message.ack()

topic_path = 'projects/ourproject/topics/test12345'
subscription_path = 'projects/ourproject/subscriptions/test12345'

publisher = pubsub.PublisherClient()
publisher.create_topic(topic_path)

subscriber = pubsub.SubscriberClient()
subscriber.create_subscription(subscription_path, topic_path)
subscription = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_path)

future = subscription.open(callback)
result = future.result()
```
^^^ this is where it hangs forever, although it continues to print messages when the callback runs, on each receive.  originally we thought it was because we're using a ThreadPool and calling these via `.map()`, it might be deadlocked somehow, but after reduction to a toy demo with single thread, single subscriber, no other work being done, it's still blocking forever.  here's the google lib versions:

```
 $ pip freeze | grep google
google-api-core==0.1.2
google-auth==1.2.1
google-auth-oauthlib==0.2.0
google-cloud-core==0.28.0
google-cloud-pubsub==0.29.2
google-compute-engine==2.7.1
googleapis-common-protos==1.5.3
grpc-google-iam-v1==0.11.4
```

are we using the interface incorrectly? is `future.result()` supposed to block forever? if we're supposed to do *all* the work in the subscriber callback, and just use `result` to catch exceptions later, then how do we actually exit the blocking call when all the work is done? we have noticed that doing our own `.set_result()` in the handler does cause `.result()` to return, are we supposed to be setting the result ourselves like this?

not sure if this is user error, or a bug.  I see the patch in #3888 that seems to be the one adding this feature of returning the Future, and the following quote:

> I do think it is fine to have a future that never has a result (or only gets one if you explicitly close)

so I'm guessing that we're using it wrong and supposed to only use this for exceptions.  If so, suggest some language might be added to the docs, that the Future should *not* be used to wait on messages themselves, but actually to wait on closure of the subscription.

issue_title: Automagically convert dicts to datastore entities.
issue_body:
I just ran into the following error for the X'th time:

```python
    client.put(entity)
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/client.py", line 384, in put
    self.put_multi(entities=[entity])
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/client.py", line 408, in put_multi
    current.put(entity)
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/batch.py", line 199, in put
    _assign_entity_to_pb(entity_pb, entity)
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/batch.py", line 319, in _assign_entity_to_pb
    bare_entity_pb = helpers.entity_to_protobuf(entity)
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/helpers.py", line 219, in entity_to_protobuf
    _set_protobuf_value(value_pb, value)
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/helpers.py", line 420, in _set_protobuf_value
    _set_protobuf_value(i_pb, item)
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/helpers.py", line 408, in _set_protobuf_value
    attr, val = _pb_attr_value(val)
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/helpers.py", line 325, in _pb_attr_value
    raise ValueError("Unknown protobuf attr type %s" % type(val))
ValueError: Unknown protobuf attr type <class 'dict'>
```

The cause is pretty obvious, the following code snippet triggers this error:

```python
entity = datastore.Entity(client.key('Resource'))
entity.update(foo={'bar': 'baz'})
client.put(entity)
```

The code crashes, because `dict` type properties are not supported. Instead they should be converted to an `Entity`.

I think it's cumbersome having to convert `dict` types to `Entity` types every time. I've written a function that does the conversion recursively for production code, but for simple scripts this is still an issue.

I think it would be nice if this library would just do the conversion from `dict` to `Entity` automagically.

issue_title: Pub/Sub API lists the wrong (slightly incompatible) type for Message.attributes
issue_body:
When a subscriber receives a message, the API says it has an 'attributes' member that is a dict:

https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/11e310ab9f63f06252ff2b9ada201fd8c11ce06c/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/subscriber/message.py#L36

However, it is not, in fact, a dict; it is a protobuf map. The behaviour is quite different, which the Python protobuf API documentation explains at some length:

https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/docs/reference/python-generated#map-fields

This is not a small difference... the semantic gap is substantial, because using the `.attributes` type in place of a dict breaks code like this:

```
message_rev = None
if some_condition:
  try:
    message_rev = int(message.attributes['revision'])
  except KeyError:
    message_rev = DEFAULT
```

In this case, instead of assigning the value `DEFAULT`, this code simply raises a new exception that doesn't happen with dicts (it's a ValueError, because the try-block will execute `int('')`). This is an incompatible, breaking change.

Again, this is a subtle, but important, API difference. At a minimum, it seems like the PubSub API docs should point to the Protobuf docs, instead of simply listing the incorrect type.

issue_title: pex shadows pkg_resources, which can break newer versions of setuptools
issue_body:
with a version of setuptools inside pex's allowed range:

```
[omerta pants (kwlzn/pantsd_bugfixes)]$ pex "setuptools<34"
Python 2.7.10 (default, Dec 16 2015, 14:09:45) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.0.2 (clang-700.1.81)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
(InteractiveConsole)
>>> from setuptools.sandbox import run_setup
>>> 
```

with a newer one:

```
[omerta pants (kwlzn/pantsd_bugfixes)]$ pex setuptools==36.2.7
Python 2.7.10 (default, Dec 16 2015, 14:09:45) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.0.2 (clang-700.1.81)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
(InteractiveConsole)
>>> from setuptools.sandbox import run_setup
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/private/var/folders/bb/msvj_4_s741g93nzgrj19twc0000gn/T/tmp1zkDfP/.deps/setuptools-36.2.7-py2.py3-none-any.whl/setuptools/sandbox.py", line 15, in <module>
    import pkg_resources.py31compat
ImportError: No module named py31compat
>>> 
```

because:

```
>>> import pkg_resources
>>> pkg_resources.__file__
'/private/var/folders/bb/msvj_4_s741g93nzgrj19twc0000gn/T/tmpsTSy8w/.bootstrap/pkg_resources/__init__.pyc'
>>> 
```

Currently pex only supports abi types like 'dmu' for python 3: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/f5721a98f21dc680a0f541cede1afa724d2a4159/pex/pep425.py#L102

I built a numpy wheel on linux, and it has filename numpy-1.10.4-cp27-cp27mu-linux_x86_64.whl. Because there is no support for abi type mu for python 2.7, pex doesn't think it's a valid package for numpy on linux_x86_64.

issue_title: Error message from Zinc: No processor claimed any of these annotations
issue_body:
Captured from this discussion: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pants-devel/B7bEOQjiXDM

We are getting these errors on every build.

```
warning: No processor claimed any of these annotations: com.squareup.multipass.client.webservice.CsrfToken,javax.ws.rs.Produces,com.squareup.sake.rpc.annotation.ServerPort,com.squareup.config.AppName,com.google.inject.Inject,javax.ws.rs.Path,com.squareup.webservice.security.annotations.AllowInsecure,com.squareup.service.container.rpc.annotation.ConsumedServices,com.squareup.webservice.security.annotations.AllowUnauthorized,com.squareup.service.container.webservice.annotation.ExposedWebResources,javax.ws.rs.Consumes,com.squareup.config.ConfigValue,com.squareup.service.container.Application,javax.ws.rs.HeaderParam,com.squareup.service.container.rpc.annotation.Incoming,javax.ws.rs.core.Context,org.junit.experimental.categories.Category,com.squareup.service.container.components.Component,com.squareup.service.container.rpc.annotation.CurrentUser,com.squareup.sake.rpc.annotation.CallHandlerMap,com.squareup.service.container.cronjobs.annotations.CronJobs,javax.ws.rs.PathParam,javax.ws.rs.GET,org.junit.runner.RunWith,javax.inject.Singleton,javax.inject.Inject,com.squareup.service.container.admin.AdminConsole,javax.inject.Qualifier,com.google.inject.servlet.RequestScoped,com.squareup.service.container.core.webservice.ServiceUriPaths,javax.annotation.Nullable,com.sun.jersey.spi.resource.Singleton,org.junit.Before,com.squareup.service.container.AdminResource,javax.ws.rs.POST,com.squareup.service.container.testing.ContainerTestRunner.App,com.squareup.service.container.rpc.server.RpcServerComponentModule.ServerConfig,org.junit.Test,com.squareup.service.container.persistence.annotation.Persistence,com.squareup.multipass.client.component.Multipass,com.google.inject.Provides,com.squareup.service.container.AppModules,com.google.common.annotations.VisibleForTesting,com.squareup.webservice.JettyThreadPool
```

So I did a spot check. There is a processor for com.squareup.service.container.Application, and it is configured.

```
$ ./pants depmap <target>
...

                internal-service.container.annotation-processors.src.main.java.app-config-processor
...
```

I see that with JMake and the twitter commons Compiler, there is a `-Tnowarnregex` option that was suppressing these before.   @stuhood reports that Twitter filed an internal bug for it.

Consensus in the group discussion is that we should do something similar in zinc that we did with jmake.  Maybe this time build something into pants to ignore certain error message output instead of passing an argument to zinc.

issue_title: Bundle shouldn't cause the invocation of binary
issue_body: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/5704 introduced `bundle.py`, which depends on `pex_archives`. That product is produced by `python_binary_create`, which is registered under binary. This causes binary to be invoked when bundle is used, even if no python targets are in scope.

issue_title: Server-side encryption on by default breaks with some S3 providers

It seems that wal-e is requesting SSE encryption by default https://github.com/wal-e/wal-e/blob/master/wal_e/blobstore/s3/s3_util.py#L57

This is problematic for any S3 backend that does not support SSE without significant configuration. For example the last couple of releases of Minio (popular self-hosting S3 solution) would reject WALE operations requesting SSE encryption requests unless a very specific KMS is configured.

issue_title: Add support for new transit information
issue_body: http://googlegeodevelopers.blogspot.com.au/2015/01/transit-directions-improvements.html

Some tokens that we can expect in Latin unicode address strings are entirely non-word, non-punctuation characters:
```
In [21]: addr = u'1711 N 6th ½ St #100'

In [22]: tokens = tokenize(addr)

In [23]: tokens
Out[23]: [u'1711', u'N', u'6th', u'\xbd', u'St', u'#', u'100']

In [24]: re.sub(r'(^[\W]*)|([^.\w]*$)', u'', tokens[3])  # token_clean
Out[24]: u''
```
This empty string for `token_clean` [results in an IndexError here](https://github.com/datamade/usaddress/blob/master/usaddress/__init__.py#L142):
```
134: UnicodeWarning: Unicode equal comparison failed to convert both arguments to Unicode - interpreting them as being unequal
  if token in (u'&', u'#') :
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
IndexError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
[...]
 in tokenFeatures(token)
    138     token_abbrev = re.sub(r'[.]', u'', token_clean.lower())
    139     features = {'nopunc' : token_abbrev,
--> 140                 'abbrev' : token_clean[-1] == u'.',
    141                 'case' : casing(token_clean),
    142                 'digits' : digits(token_clean),

IndexError: string index out of range
```
Would you consider adding vulgar fractions to the list of [single-character tokens](https://github.com/datamade/usaddress/blob/master/usaddress/__init__.py#L136), or passing the entire token through unidecode first? Let me know if a pull request would help.

usaddress.parse('JANET S SMITH 7710 MAIN AVE APT 1110 LOS ANGELES CA 9151-2100 ')

Appears to be where the name could be a street name and the middle initial is directional (N, S, E, W).

Output looks like
JANET, SubaddressType
S, SubaddressIdentifier
SMITH, SubaddressType

Using usaddress.tag fails, since there are two SubaddressType seperated by SubaddressIdentifier. 

Perhaps if there is a sequence of SubaddressType > SubaddressIdentifier > SubaddressType > followed by valid address tags, to assign bigger weight towards that combo being a Recipient rather than Subaddress.

issue_title: last fails if table has no created_at column
Combine dbbackup and dbrestore to a dbrecover command
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Perhaps because of older SQLite pragma settings (which are non-reversible btw), we are running into a lot of database corruptions ATM. Other probably causes are unclean shutdowns or migrations, or concurrent access (Iceqube?).

One aspect of this issue is to research why, the other is to find a remedy that's better than to leave the user with an application crash and a traceback.

1. [ ] Research if possible to create a command `dbrecover` that essentially does `dbbackup` and `dbrestore --latest`
1. [ ] Catch exception at appropriate place and show suggestion to use `dbrecover` (or `sqlite3 <path> ".dump" | sqlite3 <path>`)
1. [ ] Find out why we have these somewhat frequent errors.

### Expected behavior

Yes, the database can be crashed / malformed. We can never entirely rule that out.

### User-facing consequences

A user may decide to delete their entire database to get Kolibri running. Or might have to spend time on the internet. Or doesn't have internet, so will never solve the problem. It's pretty bad.

### Errors and logs

```
File “/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/kolibri/dist/django/db/backends/sqlite3/base.py”, line 323, in execute
return Database.Cursor.execute(self, query, params)
django.db.utils.DatabaseError: database disk image is malformed```
-->

### Steps to reproduce

@radinamatic has tried it while running out of disk space, I have tried it during an upgrade from 0.7.1 to 0.7.2

Let's post some broken databases.

### Context

Well, any OS and possibly any version of Kolibri.

issue_title: Add option to specify an output file for cdxj
issue_body:
The current approach to generate the index file is to use it something like this:

```
$ ipwb index sample.warc > sample.cdxj
```

This works well, but there are situations where access to the `stdout` or redirecting it to a file is not easy. Also, when the indexer is being run along with some other things, the combined output on the `stdout` may generate an invalid index. This has been the case when I tried using it with Docker for example. Here is what I am looking for now, while keeping the default output on `stdout`, if `-o` or `--output` argument is passed with a filename (with any path), that file should be used for writing the index instead. Something like this:

```
$ ipwb index -o sample.cdxj sample.warc
```

issue_title: Memento endpoint no longer resolves embedded resources
issue_body: `ipwb index ipwb/samples/warcs/froggie.warc.gz | ipwb replay` then access `http://127.0.0.1:5000/20170301192639/cs.odu.edu/~mkelly/semester/2017_spring/remotefroggie.html` in a browser--the image is broken. The logic in `/memento/` endpoint no longer resolves requests for the resource at the nearest datetime, thus the image that should be resolved from `http://127.0.0.1:5000/memento/http://matkelly.com/froggies/frog.png` as rerouted by the service worker never resolves.

issue_title: STDOUT print does not appear in docker log
issue_body: By default Python buffers the output, that's why small amount of output of `print` function (on default `STDOUT`) is not logged. This can either be fixed by supplying `-u` flag to `python` command of by setting `PYTHONUNBUFFERED` environment variable.

issue_title: Function metadata should include its name
issue_body:
functions.py is organized as a dictionary, where the key is the C function name and the value is its metadata.

This has a couple of drawbacks:

* It's inconsistent with parameters
* It doesn't allow us to control ordering in metadata
* We can't pass a function dictionary alone if we need the function name

This proposes changing functions.py to be a list of dictionaries, each element corresponding to a function. The C name would be one of the key/value pairs in the dictionary.

issue_body:
Hi there,
as per https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/topics/i18n/timezones/#code it seems like the date-time passed to the version_end_date and version_start_date are being passed a naive datetime (that is a datetime without a timezone).

> [...] refactor your code wherever you instantiate datetime objects to make them aware. This can be done incrementally. django.utils.timezone defines some handy helpers for compatibility code: now(), is_aware(), is_naive(), make_aware(), and make_naive().

I think this happens because of https://github.com/swisscom/cleanerversion/blob/master/versions/models.py#L51 but i'm not sure.

Thanks!

issue_title: Container cannot start if etcd does not listen on 127.0.0.1 address
issue_body:
As it is advised, on our hosts etcd listens on only the private network addresses (10.x.x.x:4001), but in this case it is not able to start any container, because in the generated ServiceUnit files there are no `-C $ETCD` option for the `etcdctl` commands, so the image cannot be found.

Like:
```
[Unit]
Description=lambda-unicycle_v2.cmd.1

[Service]
ExecStartPre=/bin/sh -c "IMAGE=$(etcdctl get /deis/registry/host 2>&1):$(etcdctl get /deis/registry/port 2>&1)/lambda-unicycle:v2; docker pull $IMAGE"
ExecStartPre=/bin/sh -c "docker inspect lambda-unicycle_v2.cmd.1 >/dev/null 2>&1 && docker rm -f lambda-unicycle_v2.cmd.1 || true"
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "IMAGE=$(etcdctl get /deis/registry/host 2>&1):$(etcdctl get /deis/registry/port 2>&1)/lambda-unicycle:v2; port=$(docker inspect -f '{{range $k, $v := .ContainerConfig.ExposedPorts }}{{$k}}{{end}}' $IMAGE | cut -d/ -f1) ; docker run --name lambda-unicycle_v2.cmd.1    -P -e PORT=$port $IMAGE "
ExecStop=/usr/bin/docker stop lambda-unicycle_v2.cmd.1
ExecStop=/usr/bin/docker rm -f lambda-unicycle_v2.cmd.1
TimeoutStartSec=20m
TimeoutStopSec=10
RestartSec=5
Restart=on-failure
```

I guess it is generated via the https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/controller/scheduler/fleet.py file.

issue_title: TelemetryClient (and likely others) never stop trying to send
issue_body:
I've been investigating https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/4649

The summary is that our outgoing internet proxy is blocking telemetry requests, and this is causing the upload processes to hang around in an infinite loop every time the `az` CLI is executed.

I've traced this problem down to https://github.com/Microsoft/ApplicationInsights-Python/blob/master/applicationinsights/channel/SenderBase.py#L123-L146

Any time a request fails, it is re-queued for another attempt. There is no code which ever causes this to give up, which is especially problematic when a request will never succeed.


Any thoughts on how this could/should be resolved?
Perhaps a counter should be put on the queue items that can give up after a certain number of failures?
This looks like it could be tricky to implement, as I can't see an envelope on the queue item where this metadata could live.

We are thinking that is it a better way to reset dataframe index while calculating index mass? So that  the np.argmax will get the the index regardless of which part of the whole sample is the sample from  but just the position of the time in the series.

issue_title: Get rid of code duplication between FastChainSyncer and LightPeerChain
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

They both implement logic to sync block headers, triggered by peer announcements

### How can it be fixed

Although one uses the LES sub-protocol and the other uses ETH, we should be able to abstract that by having `BasePeer` provide a standard API, and then we can reuse `FastChainSyncer` in `LightPeerChain`

### What is wrong?

There are many places in the codebase that use the check `if computation.error` to check for whether a given round of computation resulted in an error.  This would be better implemented as a dedicated API so that future changes to the EVM semantics on what constitutes an error are not directly tied to the presence or absence or truthyness of what lives at `computation.error`


### How can it be fixed

Add a these new APIs to the `Computation` object:

* `Computation.is_success`
* `Computation.is_error`

`is_success` should be a computed `property` that returns `True` if `computation.error is None`.  Conversely,  `is_error` should be a computed property that returns the opposite of whatever `is_success` returns.

### What is wrong?

PoW verification is computationally expensive (even for high-performant languages like Go)

### How can it be fixed

instead of verifying the PoW on every header, geth does so on a randomly chosen subset of them, in a way that the
probability of a forgery is smaller than the probability of an attacker producing a SHA3 collision.

issue_title: LightPeerChain must not cancel() its peers on exit
issue_body: LightPeerChain calls `.cancel()` on all connected peers when it is shutting down. That is the job of `PeerPool`'s shutdown routine

issue_title: `sys.setrecursionlimit` is hard coded but should use `constants.STACK_DEPTH_LIMIT`
issue_body:
**Problem**: presently `STACK_DEPTH_LIMIT` is defined dynamically in `constants` module but hard coded in `__init__.py`: 
https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/c54c8c2bdc3feea45659e58b86bc7c58ab0ade38/evm/__init__.py#L30

**Solution**: i believe should simply `from .constant import STACK_DEPTH_LIMIT` and use in call to `sys.setrecursionlimit`

issue_title: Add support of recent versions of Windows
issue_body:
At the moment `cfgrib` supports only Linux and MacOS.

For Windows we need:
- ecCodes support (experimental)
- CFFI support and DLL search capabilities
- popular Python distributions

issue_title: `conda update --all` wants to downgrade from conda-canary (and then things go haywire)
issue_body:
I just did the following to try out conda-canary:

```
>conda config --add channels conda-canary
>conda update conda
...
The following packages will be UPDATED:

    conda: 4.1.9-py35_0 --> 4.2.1-py35_0 conda-canary

Proceed ([y]/n)? y
...
```

I then thought I might update all the packages, and conda wants to downgrade itself:

```
>conda update --all

I'm not sure why upgrading via `conda update conda` would produce a different result for the package than `conda update --all`, intuitively the latter feels like it's a superset of the former, and the channel priority logic should apply the same way to both.

I sometimes want to put conda environments in docker containers.  Unfortunately, installing the classic scientific Python stack results in docker image sizes that are easily 1-3GB in size, which can be problematic when moving them around. 

### Example environment

Here is an example environment that I care about today:

    conda create -n test-defaults  cytoolz dask distributed fastparquet git ipywidgets jupyterlab matplotlib nb_conda_kernels netcdf4 nomkl numba numpy pandas python-blosc scipy xarray zict

This takes up 1.3GB of space.  

```
mrocklin@carbon:~/Software/anaconda/envs$ du -hs test-defaults/
1.3G	test-defaults/
```

I'm curious what all parts of the community can do to reduce this amount, including both package builders and library authors.  

### Example offenders, pandas and scipy

Some of the biggest offenders include `scipy`, and `pandas`.  

```
mrocklin@carbon:~/Software/anaconda/envs$ du -hs test-defaults/lib/python3.6/site-packages/{scipy,pandas}
62M	test-defaults/lib/python3.6/site-packages/scipy
53M	test-defaults/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pandas
```

This is worse on conda-forge

```
mrocklin@carbon:~/Software/anaconda/envs$ du -hs test-conda-forge/lib/python3.6/site-packages/{scipy,pandas}
148M	test-size/lib/python3.6/site-packages/scipy
109M	test-size/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pandas
```

And I suspect that this does not fully encompass the problem.  Anecdotally I've noticed that avoiding and then installing scipy results in a 400MB increase, so presumably there are a number of dependency packages that were brought on.

### pip

Pip packages seem to be smaller, but are still unpleasantly large.  An environment similar to the one above consumes around 800MB on my machine.

### What's going on?

Is all of this compiled code?  If so is it expected to get hundreds of megabytes of binary artifacts?  Even given the code complexity of these projects it surprises me that they can not be expressed more concisely than hundreds of megabytes.  Are we including some dependencies multiple times?  Are we accidentally shipping test data around?  What is going on?  How can we help reduce the size, in bytes, of the scipy stack?

Another one from chatting to @tswicegood at pycon:

Have the channel api be a REST request thing where you ask a channel what it knows about a package, eg:

```
GET /channel/mypackage

{..., 'platforms': ['0.5.0x', '10.1.1']}
```

So:

- be able to look up a package, rather than having to download the 'total set of available package'

- have a compact notation (the two element list above) for the range of available versions available, so you know whether this channel can serve you the version you require

issue_title: It would be nice if conda suggested to install from conda instead of the OS's package manager
issue_body:
In this case:

```
(pelican)moorepants@caramelmonkey:eme150a-website(master)$ ipython
The program 'ipython' is currently not installed. You can install it by typing:
sudo apt-get install ipython
```

it would be nice if the suggestion was from conda instead of apt because I'm in a conda environment (at least for packages that are available in the current channels).

In a conda meta.yaml file it makes a difference if a dependency in `requirements` is written 

`numpy >= 0.1` or  `numpy >=0.1`. 

The first one fails if executed with `conda build`: `conda.exceptions.InvalidSpecError: Invalid spec: >=`. 

I think one more space or not should not make a difference. 

Can this behaviour be changed?

At the same time, if you define a `requirements.txt` file like 

```
numpy >= 0.1
scipy >= 0.1
```

and you use it with `conda install --file requirements.txt` the one more space is fine.

issue_title: Consider translating core pieces of conda to a compiled language
issue_body:
Core conda code running under a compiled binary would have potential security benefits, performance benefits, and use-case flexibility benefits.  This issue specifically targets the unlink-link transaction and package extraction (decompress and untar) translated to a compiled language library.  That library could optionally be compiled to a single-file executable with all dependencies (e.g. compression algorithms) statically linked.  High-level interfaces for conda of course would still be python, but internally conda would interact with the library through standard python extensions, cffi, or another of the several available methods.

A primary beneficiary of this effort would be the constructor project, where a full python interpreter would no longer be required to be embedded in each installer, nor would the installer have a requirement of pre-existing system dependencies, like a posix shell and bzip2.

😿

A valid repo must minimally contain a file (even if blank) `noarch/repodata.json` and `noarch/repodata.json.bz2`.  Right now, conda assumes that the platform directory for a channel always exists, and it causes errors when it doesn't.  We'll likely be adding new platforms soon, and noarch is/should be in all cases the common `subdir`.  anaconda.org is already compatible:
- https://conda.anaconda.org/kalefranz/noarch/repodata.json
- https://conda.anaconda.org/kalefranz/label/main/noarch/repodata.json
- https://conda.anaconda.org/kalefranz/label/NOPE/noarch/repodata.json

The changes needed to be made will mostly affect `conda/core/index.py` and `fetch_repodata`.  Right now, we assume a `404` response on `/noarch/repodata.json` is ok.  We simply need to flip the assumption, and also make sure we have good error messaging for people with their own repos.

I want to stress that this change is to enable easier expansion of conda's supported platforms and architectures.

issue_title: PyPI tarball is missing tests directory and doesn't match github release tarball
issue_body: The github v5.0.6 tar.gz release tarball contains the tests directory, but the version of the release on PyPI does not.  These two tarballs do not match.  They should be sha checksum identical, otherwise it will be unclear which is the definitive release.

Add a function that takes a variant and returns ISCN coordinates. See #443

Now that Scout requires python version 3.6 (since ModuleNotFoundError is new in the line below), I though it might be helpful to write that version requirement on the github frontpage or somewhere else visible.

Perhaps the line below could even have a try-except clause with a hint printed to help people realize that the reason they get an error is that their python is too old - I found realized it relatively quickly, but with a bit of luck (I looked up the Error in the [docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#ModuleNotFoundError) and saw that it was new in version 3.6).

Currently, when accessing the `.qual` attribute of an AlignedSegment, the full sequence of quality values is computed every time. This results in unnecessarily bad performance especially when accessing `read.qual[i]` for more than one i. (See also the report on the mailing list from June 1).

issue_title: [Enhancement] Let the advanced user choose the full path to the compiler
issue_body:
As a follow up to #128 

> I don't remember why I chose to do it that way (I wrote the compiler code two years ago) but indeed atm you can only choose a directory. I think there is no need to do it that way anymore so I might change that for v4.7 (maybe open a new issue that request for the full compiler path). 

As you may guess I explicit ask for changing this in 4.6.2 as 4.7 is a long way to go - and I'd expect most users to switch to the new IDE as it's target to be release on the same time.

### Description

Wanted to copy a text and pressed [CTRL] while marking a text

issue_title: [Question] Recommend way to get a raw string value from a custom query
issue_body: Since the JSON output type will return quoted value strings (e.g. `"foo"` instead of `foo`), what is the recommended solution for getting the raw/unquoted value of a string that is returned from using a custom `--query` flag? Either way, I'd love to hear what the recommended solution is for this, since I'm probably missing something.

issue_title: [App Service] [Discussion] Shorten appservice command group name to just "app"
issue_body:
I'd love to discuss whether we could shorten the `appservice` command group to just `app` (or equivalent)  in order to simplify typing it out so frequently. The use of `az` instead of "Azure" feels so fluid to type (as does nsg, vm, nic, etc.), and I wonder whether we couldn't apply a similar naming abbreviation for App Service, in order to improve the efficiency of using its command on a regular basis. 

Maybe it's just me, but `az app` sounds and flows really well, and saving seven keystrokes for every operation would go a long way. Apologies if this conversation has already happened. I just wanted to throw it out there while the App Service module was still in beta, and could potentially make a breaking change like this.

issue_title: [Storage] Resource group should be optional or omitted for most storage management commands
issue_body: Since storage accounts have globally unique names, there is no reason you should have to specify a resource group name when performing storage account management operations (except create). In KeyVault, the vault is found using only the name provided it is contained within the subscription. If resource group IS specified, it is treated as a filter (i.e. perform this operation only if the vault with X name is in Y resource group).

issue_title: Availability zone support for the APIs
issue_body:
Currently, when we create a snapshot, we do it like this.

```
compute_client.snapshots.create_or_update(
                self.resource_group,
                snapshot_name,
                {
                    'location': disk_info.location,
                    'tags': tags,
                    'creation_data': {
                        'create_option': DiskCreateOption.copy,
                        'source_uri': disk_info.id
                    }
                }
            )
```

Similarly, for a volume creation, we do it this way

```
compute_client.disks.create_or_update(
                    self.resource_group,
                    disk_name,
                    {
                        'location': self.instance_location,
                        'tags': self.tags,
                        'creation_data': {
                            'create_option': DiskCreateOption.copy,
                            'source_uri': snapshot.id
                        }
                    }
                )
```

We were trying to use the availability zone preview version. But the library at this point do not support it. We have the following questions.

1. Is there a way we can pass the availability zone information for disk creation?
2. Our assumption is that snapshot is not a zonal entity. Which would mean that I can create disk in any AZ from a snapshot. Is that understanding correct?
3. Is there a plan or timeline when AZ support will be available for the library?

Regards,
Subhankar

issue_title: Move Cronbox out of Chef into Lambda
issue_body:
Currently, we are running a dedicated EC2 instance configured with Chef for Cron only. To complete our move to Docker, we want to get this functionality out of system-level Cron and into scheduled Cloudwatch events. There are two kinds of scheduled tasks:

- Dotmap updates, zip collections, and other big tasks are implemented as whole-instance processes configured via EC2 user data (in #614 we switched to Docker for these). They are kicked off via `openaddr-run-ec2-command` configures and starts a single-purpose EC2 instance.
- Coverage page is updated hourly with a task that does not run on a dedicated instance, but instead is called as `openaddr-sum-up-data` directly. This can’t be run on Lambda itself unless we drastically change the installation process for OpenAddresses, and its hourly run is too frequent to make economic sense on EC2.

The first group is pretty easy, and can be run easily and directly from Lambda if appropriate IAM role permissions are in place. The Coverage data may be more complex, and could be handled as a dedicated instance if run less frequently, e.g. daily.

The cronbox is no longer doing anything. All of the jobs have been moved elsewhere.

- [ ] Delete `chef`, `Vagrantfile`, and friends
- [ ] Update `docs` with up-to-date information
- [ ] Clean up `scripts` and `ops` with usage hints
- [ ] Bumped major version number to 6.x

Hi everyone,

It looks like that from commit f66a053e7f6b1081f000cd871b73b04ea4a62433, zero transition probability is ignored. It was actually a useful tool for disabling transitions between specific states.

It's caused by this line which assigns `0` log probability (instead of `-inf` log probability) to zero transition probability:
``` 
with np.errstate(divide='ignore'):
    _log_prob = np.where(prob > 0, np.log(prob), 0)
```

It can be solved by keeping the original value:
```
with np.errstate(divide='ignore'):
    _log_prob = np.log(prob)
```

What do you think?

Cheers,
David

Hello,
I am using janus, very happily, to mix async and threaded code, and it works great.

I have just a little suggestion about the API: I think you should provide a `head()` or `peek()` method, that allows to get the first element in the queue without removing it. Although this is not really necessary (also, you provide a `PriorityQueue`), I think it's a great addition which could make user code easier.

The current `get()` method could be implemented using `head()` + removal, so code should stay reasonably similar to the current.

This would allow to take decisions earlier, without messing with queue order.

issue_title: Client request tracing. Enable passing variables from context to TracingConfig
issue_body:
## Long story short

(this is discussion moved from https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/2685)

I would like to be able to do the following:
```
        pivots = [{'name': 'balancing_group_id', 'value': group.id}]
        tracer = create_tracer_instance(self.app, pivots)
        async with self.client.trace_configs([tracer]):
            async with self.client.get(url) as resp:
                ...

```
By allowing this I can pass it context-specific variables into the `TraceConfig` instance. This would allow me to save them in a way that will later allow to filter them by the specified params.

@pfreixes has pointed out, that `ClientSession` allows passing in `trace_request_ctx` through the interface of get/post/put/delete method. 

My opinion is that it is not sufficient, because will not play nice with the third-party libraries, because high-level methods they expose would have to be update to funnel `trace_request_ctx`.


## Implementation idea (pseudo-code, not tested)

```
diff --git a/aiohttp/client.py b/aiohttp/client.py
index 93eff65..b04abf0 100644
--- a/aiohttp/client.py
+++ b/aiohttp/client.py
@@ -139,10 +138,25 @@ class ClientSession:
         self._response_class = response_class
         self._ws_response_class = ws_response_class

-        self._trace_configs = trace_configs or []
-        for trace_config in self._trace_configs:
+        self.task_locals = TaskLocals(loop=loop)
+        self._static_trace_configs = trace_configs
+        for trace_config in trace_configs:
             trace_config.freeze()

+    def _get_trace_configs(self):
+        return self.task_locals.get('trace_configs', []) + self._static_trace_configs
+
+    def _set_trace_configs(self, tracing_configs):
+        self.task_locals.set('trace_configs', tracing_configs)
+        for trace_config in trace_configs:
+            trace_config.freeze()
+
+    @async_contextmanager
+    async def trace_configs(self, trace_config):
+        self.set_trace_configs(tracing_configs)
+        yield
+        self.set_trace_configs([])
+
     def __init_subclass__(cls):
         warnings.warn("Inheritance class {} from ClientSession "
                       "is discouraged".format(cls.__name__),
@@ -256,7 +270,7 @@ class ClientSession:
                 trace_config.trace_config_ctx(
                     trace_request_ctx=trace_request_ctx)
             )
-            for trace_config in self._trace_configs
+            for trace_config in self._get_trace_configs()
         ]

         for trace in traces:
```

issue_title: Forcibly closed connection to web.Application results in infinite loop of Exceptions that cannot be caught
issue_body:
## Long story short

When the TCP connection to my websocket server is forcibly closed (outside Python's control, such as with TCPView), there is an infinite loop of Exceptions that my user-level code cannot catch.

## Expected behaviour

I would expect to get a connection closed message or exception as with other kinds of service interruptions.

## Actual behaviour

I get an infinite loop of exceptions that cannot be caught.

    Exception in callback BaseSelectorEventLoop._read_from_self()
    handle: <Handle BaseSelectorEventLoop._read_from_self()>
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "C:\Python37-32\lib\asyncio\events.py", line 88, in _run
        self._context.run(self._callback, *self._args)
      File "C:\Python37-32\lib\asyncio\selector_events.py", line 125, in _read_from_self
        data = self._ssock.recv(4096)
    ConnectionResetError: [WinError 10054] An existing connection was forcibly closed by the remote host


## Steps to reproduce

The code below is a smallest-representation of the problem.  Run the websocket server on localhost, and connect to it with a client such as the tool at http://www.websocket.org/echo.html?location=ws://localhost:9999 .  Then forcibly close the TCP connection using a tool like TCPView to simulate an OS-level event.

    #!/usr/bin/env python3
    import asyncio
    import webbrowser
    
    from aiohttp import web
    
    
    async def setup():
        app = web.Application()
        app.router.add_get("/", ws_handler)
        runner = web.AppRunner(app)
        await runner.setup()
        site = web.TCPSite(runner, "localhost", port=9999)
        await site.start()
    
    
    async def ws_handler(request: web.BaseRequest):
        ws = web.WebSocketResponse()
        try:
            await ws.prepare(request)
            await ws.send_str("Welcome to this demo.")
            await ws.send_str("Trying forcing closed this connection with something like TCPView:")
            await ws.send_str("https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sysinternals/downloads/tcpview")
            while not ws.closed:
                ws_msg = await ws.receive()
                print("Received:", ws_msg)
        except Exception as ex:
            print("I cannot catch the Exception when connection is forcibly closed.", flush=True)
            print("Exception", ex, flush=True)
            ex.with_traceback()
        finally:
            print("I am never run when connection is forcibly closed.", flush=True)
    
        return ws
    
    
    webbrowser.open("http://www.websocket.org/echo.html?location=ws://localhost:9999")
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.create_task(setup())
    loop.run_forever()
