issue_title: SSL Errors are not catched in proxy connector
issue_body:
## Long story short

I'm running an application which can connects to many heterogeneous systems. Sometimes, SSL is badly done and verify fails.

When using a proxy connector, the exception is not catched by aiohttp. So my application, which is only waiting for standard aiohttp exception, fails to catch this one.


## Expected behaviour

I expected the connector to catch this error so that it can throw an aiottp exception ClientSSLError, like the standard connector : https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/connector.py#L822

## Actual behaviour


```
future: <Task finished coro=<NodeConnector.connect_peers() done, defined at sakia\data\connectors\node.py:278> exception=SSLError(1, '[SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:719)')>

    ----
    Traceback (most recent call last):

  File "asyncio\tasks.py", line 242, in _step

  File "sakia\data\connectors\node.py", line 289, in connect_peers

  File "site-packages\aiohttp\client.py", line 692, in __aenter__

  File "site-packages\aiohttp\client.py", line 457, in _ws_connect

  File "site-packages\aiohttp\helpers.py", line 99, in __iter__

  File "site-packages\aiohttp\client.py", line 269, in _request

  File "site-packages\aiohttp\connector.py", line 392, in connect

  File "site-packages\aiohttp\connector.py", line 735, in _create_connection

  File "site-packages\aiohttp\connector.py", line 898, in _create_proxy_connection

  File "asyncio\base_events.py", line 802, in create_connection

  File "asyncio\base_events.py", line 828, in _create_connection_transport

  File "asyncio\futures.py", line 381, in __iter__

  File "asyncio\tasks.py", line 310, in _wakeup

  File "asyncio\futures.py", line 294, in result

  File "asyncio\sslproto.py", line 502, in data_received

  File "asyncio\sslproto.py", line 201, in feed_ssldata

  File "ssl.py", line 641, in do_handshake

ssl.SSLError: [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:719)
```

## Steps to reproduce

Connect to a self-signed HTTPS website, throught a proxy.

## Your environment

Windows 64 bits, python 3.5.

Sometimes servers will disconnect the client on purpose after each request. 

When using aiohttp.ClientSession to do several calls, it means we need to re-create a session for each request, and that defeats the with pattern..

I would like to propose the addition of a "reconnect" option (False by default)

when used, this option will try to reconnect if the previous call disconnected the socket, so this 
code works even if the server disconnects the client after the first call:

```python

async with aiohttp.ClientSession(headers=headers, reconnect=True) as session:
    async with session.get("http://httpbin.org/headers") as r:
       ..
    async with session.get("http://httpbin.org/headers") as r:
      ...
```  

What do you think? Happy to try to implement it of you think it's  good idea!

This bug is just a marker to let other people know that this is an issue, but not in aiohttp...so if they google for this issue they can find what the real issue is.

Per my research in https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/3933 I found that the underlying Python SSL sockets are leaking.  I logged http://bugs.python.org/issue29870 where they recently found and proposed a patch to fix this leak.  I'll close this as soon as they're released new versions of python with the fix.

I'm not sure if it's worthwhile adding a warning to let users know that certain python versions will leak when using SSL sockets.

I think the concept of solution cells is confusing, because you can just make the solution region (e.g. with `### BEGIN SOLUTION`), and so then it's less clear what the difference between solution cells and grade cells is. I propose getting rid of the solution cell checkbox, and just requiring people to use the solution region annotation. Then, in the cell toolbar, we can change the "grade?" checkbox to be different for markdown vs code cells:

* for markdown cells, the label should be something like "written answer?"
* for code cells, the label should be something like "tests for code answer?"

Under the hood, we'll still have the concept of solution cells and grade cells, but I think in the UI the different pieces are just confusing.

For my proposed new cell type (#131), we could add a checkbox that has a label saying "prevent edits?" or something like that. It would automatically get checked on code cells that are grade cells, and would be impossible to check on markdown cells that are grade cells.

@ellisonbg what are your thoughts on this?

issue_title: Use extension activate/deactivate apps from jupyter
issue_body: Rather than using our own.

Some deployments of the Jupyter notebook such as binder prefix URLs. In the case of binder, the prefix is the app id. When using require.config to specify paths for non AMD modules in nbextensions, then you

- we cannot use `/nbextensions/foobar` because this would remove the URL prefix.

    note: this is currently recommended in [the deprecation warning for static/widgets/js](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/531b6650ca3fe4008004b9977fed01d9af53d1ce/notebook/notebookapp.py#L123)
- we cannot use `nbextensions/foobar` because the configured base url is configured to `'notebook/static'`.

We are currently using SQLite, which does not like being on NFS. 
It's slightly annoying for cloud deployment. 

I would like to investigate/document making that plugable in order for large scale deployment to have a customisable solution.

It should be possible to write multiple 'build' tests in a single file to test various build options. But, for example, even duplicating/renaming the following test function fails:

https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/93aca7b67f854ab2e9dfdcaaf99cdeaea0280413/tests/test_hello.py#L13-L15

Running nsqlookupd on ports: tcp=10000; http=10001
Running nsqd on ports: tcp=10100; http=10101
Running nsqd on ports: tcp=10200; http=10201

Then I am running the following code (it writes one message, then waiting for this message to read back, then writes another message and so on).
And it is hanging forever after a few round tripped messages:

```python
import nsq
from tornado import ioloop
from tornado.queues import Queue
from tornado import gen
from uuid import uuid4
import time


class Writer(nsq.Writer):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(Writer, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self._connected_future = gen.Future()

    def push(self, topic, message):
        future = gen.Future()

        def callback(conn, result):
            if result == 'OK':
                future.set_result(result)
            else:
                future.set_exception(result)

        self.pub(topic, message, callback=callback)
        return future

    def _on_connection_ready(self, conn, **kwargs):
        super(Writer, self)._on_connection_ready(conn, **kwargs)

        if not self._connected_future.done():
            self._connected_future.set_result(None)

    def wait_for_connection(self):
        return self._connected_future


class Reader(nsq.Reader):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(Reader, self).__init__(*args, message_handler=self.__handle_message, **kwargs)
        self._messages = Queue()

    def __handle_message(self, message):
        message.enable_async()
        self._messages.put_nowait(message)

    def pop(self):
        return self._messages.get()


@gen.coroutine
def main():
    channel = topic = 'test#ephemeral'
    nsqd_addresses = [
        'localhost:10100',
        'localhost:10200'
    ]
    reader = Reader(
        topic,
        channel,
        lookupd_http_addresses='http://localhost:10001',
        lookupd_poll_interval=1,
        max_in_flight=32,
        output_buffer_timeout=1
    )
    writer = Writer(nsqd_tcp_addresses=nsqd_addresses)

    for i in xrange(10**6):

        start_time = time.time()
        yield writer.push(topic, message_text)
        message = yield reader.pop()
        message.finish()
        assert message.body == message_text
        dt = time.time() - start_time
        print '[{}] Round-trip time: {:.02f}ms'.format(i, dt * 1000)


ioloop.IOLoop.current().run_sync(main)

```

issue_title: Feature request: Add support for checking memberUid group attributes
issue_body:
By default, OpenLDAP doesn't add the `memberOf` group membership attribute to user objects. Instead, group objects typically contain lists of members, each one with a `memberUid` value in the group object. To check if a user belongs to a group, clients typically need to locate the user account and then perform a second query to see if that user is in the group (ex. `(&(objectClass=posixGroup)(cn=GROUP_NAME)(memberUid=USER_NAME))`).

matrix-synapse-ldap3 allows specifying a lookup filter when searching for user accounts, but there is no support for restricting access to groups for servers that don't store group membership information in the user objects themselves. It would be great if support for this feature were added.

issue_title: sshuttle does not work in kernel 4.9.84 or kernel 4.14.22
issue_body:
Hello

sshuttle does not work in at least kernel 4.9.84 or kernel 4.14.22 (and I suspect the other latest stable kernels).  It was broken by the following patch:

https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/stable/linux-stable.git/commit/?h=linux-4.9.y&id=4ec264d8128958e66d048f45fd1c4c28cfedb119

```
commit 4ec264d8128958e66d048f45fd1c4c28cfedb119
Author: Paolo Abeni <pabeni@redhat.com>
Date:   Tue Jan 30 19:01:40 2018 +0100

    netfilter: on sockopt() acquire sock lock only in the required scope
    
    commit 3f34cfae1238848fd53f25e5c8fd59da57901f4b upstream.
```

sshuttle appears to connect, but does not forward any traffic.  In addition, it does not close cleanly (not even with a kill -9):

```
# sshuttle -v -r remote-host 10.0.0.0/8
Starting sshuttle proxy.
firewall manager: Starting firewall with Python version 3.5.4
firewall manager: ready method name nat.
IPv6 enabled: False
UDP enabled: False
DNS enabled: False
TCP redirector listening on ('127.0.0.1', 12300).
Starting client with Python version 3.5.4
c : connecting to server...
Password:
Starting server with Python version 3.5.4
 s: latency control setting = True
c : Connected.
 s: available routes:
 s:   2/10.0.0.0/8
firewall manager: setting up.
>> iptables -t nat -N sshuttle-12300
>> iptables -t nat -F sshuttle-12300
>> iptables -t nat -I OUTPUT 1 -j sshuttle-12300
>> iptables -t nat -I PREROUTING 1 -j sshuttle-12300
>> iptables -t nat -A sshuttle-12300 -j RETURN --dest 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp
>> iptables -t nat -A sshuttle-12300 -j REDIRECT --dest 10.0.0.0/8 -p tcp --to-ports 12300 -m ttl ! --ttl 42
^CKilled by signal 2.
^C^C^C
^C
```

Reverting the kernel patch fixes the issue.

I realise this may be a kernel bug (not sure if this counts as breaking userspace...) and if so I'm happy to file a bug there instead.

Thanks
Luke

issue_title: Roslyn analyzers + code fixes don't work properly in VS when installed into .NET Core project via a nuget package
issue_body:
*(I can't seem to find a repo for raising VS .NET Core tooling issues, so if there's a more appropriate place for this, please tell and I'll be glad to repost it there.)*

Here's what happens when a nuget package that contains Roslyn analyzers + code fixes is installed into a .NET Core project via `install-package $(AnalyzerPackage)`:

1. Analyzer package is added to `References > .NETStandard` node of Solution Explorer instead of `References > Analyzers`.
2. Which also means there's no UI for changing rule severity.
3. Analyzers are not invoked when the code is being edited. The warnings are reported only when the project is built.
4. Even then no squiggles are displayed at the warning location, the warnings are only surfaced in the error list.
5. And probably the most tragic part: the code fixes attached to the analyzer warnings simply can't be triggered.

issue_title: Producer cannot be garbage collected when `group.id` set.
issue_body:
I've run into an issue where my script fails to terminate when the `group.id` config option is set on a Producer. It seems to be the case that when this option is set, the Producer cannot be garbage collected (hangs forever at the point garbage collection is attempted).

Here is a minimal script that consistently reproduces it on my environment.

``` python
import logging
import sys

# Set up logging
numeric_log_level = getattr(logging, "INFO", None)
logging.basicConfig(level=numeric_log_level, stream=sys.stdout)

from confluent_kafka import Producer

config = {
    'group.id': "something-valid"
}

logging.info("Configuration: {}".format(config))

producer = Producer(config)

logging.info("Regular producer created") # This line will run

del producer # It hangs here

logging.info("Regular producer deleted") # This line is never reached
```

issue_title: Add caching for the fallback URL packages
issue_body:
1. Add option `--fallback-cache-root` or `-c`
2. If the package is not found locally
    1. Search fallback cache root
    2. If fallback cache root doesn't have the package
        1. Retrieve the package
        2. Store it in the fallback root 
        3. Send it to the requesting client
    3. If fallback cache root does have the package
        1. Check whether package is older than `--cache-control` and if older proceed to 2.ii.a
        2. Otherwise send the cached package to the requesting client

This may very likely be a issue on matlab side, just wonder if there is a clever way to process it on matlab_kernel side.  whenever you have a large data set (e.g,a matrix that have several million row) as output and if you forget to use ";" to suppress output, it will try to copy all "millions" rows over and display it entirely. This simply freeze the whole front-end and render it useless.

```
>>>import dateutil.parser
>>>dateutil.parser.parse("2014-01")
datetime.datetime(2014, 1, 25, 0, 0)
>>>dateutil.parser.parse("2016-11")
datetime.datetime(2016, 11, 25, 0, 0)
```

I had the same issue with both Python 2 and 3. The issue is observed with python-dateutil 2.6.0.
Strangely, I remember having a similar behaviour in a previous version but with like the 8th instead of the 25th.

Maybe it's just me but this behaviour seems rather odd. If I would create a date/datetime given this format (acceptable ISO8601), I would expect it to get the 1st of the month at midnight, not a random day.

issue_title: infinite loop in write() on POSIX with write_timeout=0 (non blocking) & no space available
issue_body:
Hello serial peeps,

The serialposix.py implementation (https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/serial/serialposix.py) handles non-blocking I/O in a fairly simple way:

```
        while tx_len > 0:
            try:
                n = os.write(self.fd, d)
                if timeout.is_non_blocking:
                    # Zero timeout indicates non-blocking - simply return the
                    # number of bytes of data actually written
                    return n
                ...
            except OSError as e:
                if e.errno not in (errno.EAGAIN, errno.EALREADY, errno.EWOULDBLOCK, errno.EINPROGRESS, errno.EINTR):
                    raise SerialException('write failed: {}'.format(e))
            ...
```

However on many POSIX systems (including Linux), if the buffer is full (bytes written is 0), instead of returning 0, os.write() gives an EAGAIN result. This turns into an exception (ugh slow) which is caught around the code above -- and loops back to the main `while tx_len > 0` loop.

Until such time as there is buffer space available, this sits in a tight loop, repeating the write() call and burning 100% CPU. Not cool :-(.

Instead, in this situation (with write_timeout=0), only EINTR should cause looping. EAGAIN/EALREADY/EWOULDBLOCK/EINPROGRESS should probably all return zero, allowing the application code to handle the nonblocking write that couldn't write.

issue_body:
I have a create endpoint for a certain object. If there is a single object already in the database, when I attempt to create another one with the API, it instead updates the single existing one.

I believe this is due to the code in lines 2091-2094 in resources.py. The logic goes that if there are not existing objects in the database it raises a DoesNotExist error. If there is more than one object in the database it raises as MultipleObjectsReturned error. But if there is a single object in the database, the single item in the database is returned.

![tastypie_create_objects_issue](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/408926/22163178/99f26194-df06-11e6-9a2e-40e8e59c6ef5.png)

This returns the object to the function put_detail (line 1407), which assumes that this is an update function on that single item instead of a create function.

Instead, I would expect it to check against the existence of a PK or ID on the bundle. Perhaps by using the detail_uri_name or something like that.

issue_title: ParseSMTLIB2String does not support or throw on maximize/minimize
issue_body:
If an SMT string contains objective functions, they are swallowed silently leading to incorrect optimisation results. The parser should throw an exception to indicate they are not supported because looking at examples and guide from http://www.rise4fun.com/Z3Opt its easy to assume they are part of the model.

Ideally the parser would support objective functions allowing complete models to be parsed. If standard compliance is an issue then consider making it optional or as a ParseOptString or some such. Its desirable because if we have a text definition, we have to write a complete custom SMT parser just to parse the objective function. As far as I can see the API doesn't expose a way to parse arithmetic expressions, only bool.

[1] http://rise4fun.com/Z3Opt/tutorial/guide
_"The SMT-LIB 2.0 standard is extended with three commands for expressing optimization objectives. The (maximize t) command instructs check-sat to produce a model that maximizes the value of term t."_

 )

Thank you!

nina

issue_title: distribute-forall tactic distributes even existential quantifiers
issue_body:
The tactic `distribute-forall` distributes any quantifier (even existential) over conjunction and therefore produces incorrect results. For example given

```
(assert 
    (exists ((a (_ BitVec 32)))
          (and (= a #x00000000) (not (= a #x00000000)))
     )
)
```

the result of `(apply distribute-forall)` is

```
(goals
(goal
  (exists ((a (_ BitVec 32))) (= a #x00000000))
  (exists ((a (_ BitVec 32))) (not (= a #x00000000)))
  :precision precise :depth 1)
)
```

This transformation is not correct and causes wrong results. For example, asserting the same formula, the result of 

```
(check-sat-using (then distribute-forall smt))
(check-sat-using smt))
```

is

```
sat
unsat
```

I have tested the stable version (4.4.0) and the unstable version `ed806b6` and both have the same problem. 

I believe the way to fix this issue is to add condition `old_q->is_forall() ` to lines 34 and 56 in the file `z3/src/tactic/core/distribute_forall_tactic.cpp`.

I observed the following behaviour in Z3 4.4.0 (x64 build on Win 7 x64). Let's assume the preamble

```
(get-info :version)
(set-option :type_check true)

(declare-const b (Bool))
(declare-const x (Int))
(declare-const y (Int))
```
As expected, `(assert (= x b))` yields a `sort mismatch` error.

Unexpectedly, `(assert (= x b y))` yields no error, and a subsequent `check-sat` even yields `sat`.

Moreover, the sequence
```
(assert (= x y z))
(check-sat)
(assert (= x b y))
(check-sat)
```
makes Z3 crash (`z3.exe has stopped working`).

issue_title: Inconsistency when mixing integers and bitvectors
issue_body:
When mixing integers and bitvectors, an inconsistency can occur. See

```smt2
(define-fun UInt32ToInt ((x Int)) Int (ite (>= x 2147483648) (- x 4294967296) x))
(define-fun ToInt32 ((x Int)) (_ BitVec 32) ((_ int2bv 32) x))

(define-fun f ((a Int)) Int (UInt32ToInt (bv2int (bvor (ToInt32 (- 50)) (ToInt32 a)))))

(simplify (f 3))

(declare-const x Int)
(assert (= (- 49) (f x)))
(check-sat)
```
produces
```smt2
(- 49)
unsat
```

which is clearly contradictory. This can be run here: http://rise4fun.com/Z3/V4eI If you rearrange it so `f` just returns a bitvector rather than an integer (and we search for the bitvector equivalent to -49), we get `sat`, however. This suggests the problem is with `bv2int`.

Two limit-related issues wrt renderers:
- some renderers, like Excel, will have inherent limitations such as row counts (~65k for xls, and ~1m for xlsx)
- usually, users only render very large recordsets unintentionally (i.e. forgot to set/apply a filter, didn't notice the large number of total records, etc.)

So, we need to do two things: have the user confirm large exports, and throw a more useful exception when a hard limit is transgressed for the renderer:
- have a property on the renderer that checks the appropriate criteria and returns True/False.
  - calling the renderer when `can_render` is False should raise an exception (named something like `RenderLimitExceeded`)
  - developers may also use the property to control whether export links are shown, and provide information to the user as to how to bring the grid into an "exportable" state (e.g. apply more filters)
- place a `unconfirmed_export_limit` on the grid, set on the base grid to 10k
  - setting the attribute to None should disable the check
  - webgrid's JS should use the attribute's value to have a confirm on export links, comparing to the current record count on the grid

Currently, `Layer` parameters are assumed to be Theano shared variables. That way we can call `get_value()` and `set_value()` to them, and pass them to `theano.function` in `updates`.

However, it would be cool if `Layer` parameters could be arbitrary Theano expressions. There are a few use cases for this:

* autoencoders with tied weights. If you have two layers `l1` and `l2`, you might want to do something like `l2 = nntools.layers.DenseLayer(l1, W=l1.W.T)`. This is currently not possible because l1.W.T is not a shared variable, but rather an expression.

* sometimes the domain of parameters is restricted, and you might want to reparameterize things. For example, you might want all values to be positive. In that case you could reparameterize as follows: `l2 = nntools.layers.DenseLayer(l1, W=T.exp(V))`, where V is a shared variable. This ensures that all values in W are positive.

However, this requires some modifications to `nntools.layers.get_all_params()`, because we need a way to get the actual shared variables containing parameter values, not just the Theano expressions built on top of them. Otherwise there's no way to update them.

Given an arbitary Theano expression, it is fairly easy to get a list of all shared variables that occur in it, by traversing the Theano graph. This isn't very 'clean' I suppose, but definitely possible.

However, that just gives us a list of all shared variables, and we have no way of knowing if all of those contain learnable parameters. Perhaps some of them should not be touched by the learning.

We could assume that all shared variables represent learnable parameters by default. This would usually be the case. But then we need to provide a way for the user to specify that a given variable is not to be touched (for example, this could be a variable that contains a binary mask that restricts some of the parameters to be zero). Perhaps an extra attribute on the `Layer` instances that lists all "non-trainable" shared variables.

--

Should we support arbitrary expressions as `Layer` parameters? If so, there will be some added complexity, but it might be worth it. If we do not support this, a new `Layer` subclass has to be implemented for every new parameterization (so to support autoencoders with tied weights, we would need to implement a `TransposedDenseLayer`, for example).

What do you guys think?

I'm implementing layernormalization from http://arxiv.org/pdf/1607.06450v1.pdf
 Should i create a pr for this to lasagne? 

The feedforward variant can be implemented as separate layer similar to `BatchNormalizationLayer` and the `batch_norm` helpder. 

For GRU and LSTM i think you would need a lot of if statements to handle layer normalization because the gate_biases are no longer used and because of the 'precompute' option.

The Softmax non-linearity can lead to potential instabilities during training, LogSoftmax (i.e. log(Softmax(x)) seems to alleviate the issue. See #308 and [Theano/Theano#3162](https://github.com/Theano/Theano/issues/3162).

It might be useful to include LogSoftmax as a non-linearity and a corresponding Cross-entropy objective that assumes output probabilities are already in the log domain.

issue_title: Add ways to manipulate strings without square-bracket indexing
issue_body:
In Data8 we're trying to avoid square-bracket indexing because it confuses some students.  This makes working with strings a little challenging.  Technically, a lot can be done with the workflow `string -> list -> table -> manipulate -> list -> string`, but that's pretty ugly.  I'd like to add a few functions to `datascience` to work around this.

Concretely, here are two things I'd like to be able to do.  (Ani says some connector instructors are interested in the first one, too.)

1. Index a subrange of a string.  This could be done like `Table.take` (e.g. `item_range("foobar", np.arange(1, 5))`) or like range indexing (e.g. `item_range("foobar", 1, 5)`).  This would have the added benefit of working on NumPy arrays.

2. Have a version of the `split` method that returns an array instead of a list.  Otherwise we have to wrap calls to `split` in `np.array`, which is confusing when students have only a vague idea about the difference between lists and arrays.  Since we can't teach regular expressions, `split` is a fairly core text manipulation tool.

I realize this is not really in-scope for the `datascience` library.  Other options include avoiding text manipulation; making another library for Data8-specific helper functions; or teaching lists and square-bracket indexing.  I don't really like any of those, but I'm open to other suggestions.

The function Function.blocks calls Function._get_block (https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/c60388cddc93ec2c11a28bd0ff95b4cd5ba979f2/angr/knowledge/function.py#L103), which in turns calls self._project.factory.block.
The call to self._project.factory.block might throw AngrMemoryError, however Function.blocks only catches AngrTranslationError. The unhandled AngrMemoryError causes the analysis to terminate.

I can push the easy fix to this issue, which is catching AngrMemoryError as well. 
Before I start to do this I'd like to know whether there is a deeper bug, e.g. is it expected that a Function contains blocks which are outside of the loaded memory?

issue_title: Extract non-posix data storage from posix plugin
issue_body:
This supersedes https://github.com/angr/simuvex/issues/71.

- Add a `files` plugin to handle files and other I/O streams
- Add a `heap` plugin to handle heap layout and allocation (bonus: we can have multiple implementations of the plugin that implement multiple heap allocation/freeing strategies instead of just our one that never frees anything)
- Make posix contain the actual file descriptors as SimFileDescriptors, which point to the appropriate SimFiles
- Add a `win32` or whatever plugin that handles the windows equivalents of all this nonsense 
- Add another implementation of the `libc` plugin for the msvcrt

An important question to answer is whether we want to have posix and win32 be plugins that are named as such or if they should both be found as `state.os_env` or something like that. I'm in favor of having them named separately in order to encourage the "visible name" of a plugin to be a contract about its interface.

issue_title: Getting JSON from very old software causes a ValueError.
issue_body:
With very old (12.1X46-D60.4) software, a JunOS SRX-210 will return empty JSON, which then causes a `ValueError` exception to be raised.  This problem is an unintended consequence of issue of [issue 147](https://github.com/ncclient/ncclient/pull/147) and is incredibly arcane.

The fix I created was to enclose the call to `json.loads` in a `try..except` block to catch a `ValueError` exception.

    23,29c21,22
    <     try :
    <         payload = json.loads(result_json.xpath('.')[0].text)
    < # This exception might be thrown if the device is too old
    <     except ValueError as v:
    <         print("There was a problem getting the results in JSON format, error is %s" % v.__str__, file=sys.stderr )
    <     else :
    <         print((payload['configuration']['system']['services']))
     ---
    >     payload = json.loads(result_json.xpath('.')[0].text)
    >     print(payload['configuration']['system']['services'])

I also ran the code through 2to3, added an `import six` statement and `from __future__ import print_function` at the top of the file,  It now runs quite nicely on both python 2.7.12 and python 3.5.2

issue_body:
**Describe the bug**

Right now the `Scene` objects `save_datasets` method defaults to saving **all** currently loaded/existing datasets. This means that if a composite hasn't been generated yet, either by `generate=False` or IncompatibleAreas or some unexpected failure then `scn.save_datasets()` will save dependency datasets that weren't actually requested by the user. This can be very confusing.

**To Reproduce**

```python
scn = Scene(reader='abi_l1b', filenames=[...])
scn.load(['true_color'])
scn.save_datasets()

```

**Expected behavior**

Only true_color would be saved if it exists or it won't be saved. Optionally it could raise an exception if *none* of the requested datasets (`Scene.wishlist`) are available to save.

**Actual results**

C01, C02, C03, etc. geotiffs will be saved.

issue_title: Sql queries do not respect column orders
Allow user to return dataframes not constructed through SQL queries
issue_body: A thought I just had: Currently we only return responses as dataframes if their query is a SQL query.  Since we already have the code for parsing dataframes from JSON responses, we might provide an option that lets users say "I'm going to output a bunch of JSON here, please parse it and return it as a dataframe", even if their code isn't a SQL query.  This could be useful and could allow users to get arbitrary semi-structured data back from the remote cluster.  I'm not sure how feasible this is, or if this would be too error-prone, but I think it makes sense and could be really useful in some niche scenarios.

issue_title: Add description of new conn function 'fromList'

issue_body:
I have an application where the composition of a string and display of the localized version to the user happen in different parts at different times and I use pickle to throw around objects between them. While being not the most elegant thing ever seen, that worked perfectly fine with Flask-Babel 0.9 and speaklater 1.3.

However, since Flask-Babel 0.10 with its integrated speaklater, unpickling a LazyString gives a `RuntimeError: maximum recursion depth exceeded`:

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/flask_babel/speaklater.py", line 12, in __getattr__
    string = text_type(self)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/flask_babel/speaklater.py", line 18, in __str__
    return text_type(self._func(*self._args, **self._kwargs))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/flask_babel/speaklater.py", line 12, in __getattr__
    string = text_type(self)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/flask_babel/speaklater.py", line 18, in __str__
    return text_type(self._func(*self._args, **self._kwargs))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/flask_babel/speaklater.py", line 12, in __getattr__
    string = text_type(self)
[...]
```

This seems to happen when pickle upon unpickling tries to call `__setstate__`.  This leads to `__getattr__` being called which in turn calls `__str__` but since the object is not yet unpickled, pickle tries to do that, leading to `__getattr__` being called for `__setstate__` which wants to call `__str__`...

A minimal example to reproduce the bug would be the following:

```
import pickle

def noop(arg, *args):
    return arg

from flask_babel import LazyString # Flask-Babel 0.11.1
from speaklater import _LazyString # speaklater 1.3

s1 = _LazyString(noop, "a", {})
s2 = LazyString(noop, "a", {})
```

`s1` and `s2` look okay:

```
>>> s1
l'a'
>>> str(s1)
'a'
>>> s2
<flask_babel.speaklater.LazyString object at 0x7f7f7566fe48>
>>> str(s2)
'a'
```

Pickling and unpickling `s1` works as expected:

```
>>> p = pickle.dumps(s1)
>>> pickle.loads(p)
l'a'
```

However, trying the same thing with `s2` fails:

```
>>> p = pickle.dumps(s2)
>>> pickle.loads(p)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/flask_babel/speaklater.py", line 12, in __getattr__
    string = text_type(self)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/flask_babel/speaklater.py", line 18, in __str__
    return text_type(self._func(*self._args, **self._kwargs))
[...]
RuntimeError: maximum recursion depth exceeded
```

issue_body: The canonical homes for these are here [(track and field)](http://www.howardgrubb.co.uk/athletics/wmatnf14.html) and [here (road)](http://www.howardgrubb.co.uk/athletics/wmaroad15.html)

In `trpo_mpi.py`, we have the code:

```
def fisher_vector_product(p):
    return allmean(compute_fvp(p, *fvpargs)) + cg_damping * p
```

I'm probably missing something really obvious -- is the `allmean` needed here? The only lines which invoke this function are the `stepdir` and `shs` computations. In the following lines, we have:

```
stepdir = cg(fisher_vector_product, g, cg_iters=cg_iters, verbose=rank==0)
shs = .5*stepdir.dot(fisher_vector_product(stepdir))
```

The quantity `stepdir` appears to depends on values that are guaranteed to be identical across all threads (other than `verbose`); in particular, `g` is guaranteed to be the same across all workers, as `g = allmean(g)` before the conjugate gradient code. This means that `shs` is the same as well across all workers.

Indeed, replacing `fisher_vector_product(p)` by the following seems to work fine:

```
def fisher_vector_product(p):
    a = allmean(compute_fvp(p, *fvpargs)) + cg_damping * p
    b = compute_fvp(p, *fvpargs) + cg_damping * p
    assert np.allclose(a, b)
    return a
```

Thoughts on this?

EDIT: the `cg` method doesn't do anything different across threads either.

issue_title: Django one-to-one relation did not work
1.1.3 to 1.2.0 produces "Schema must contain unique types..."
Graphql schema probably not suited for parallel execution
issue_body:
This is probably a minor issue as it occurs only on server startup.

I noticed that my django view (with heavy use of relay), when rendered right after django startup, tends to throw a variety of graphql errors.
It came out only with relay (that is - when multiple graphql queries were sent one by one).

After diving into the code I think I more or less analyzed the issue. `GraphQLView` reuses the same schema (`graphene.core.schema.Schema`) for execution. That schema  has `self._types` dictionary, which is constructed only once.
- When more than one thread interfere during initial `_types` construction, `Type {} already registered with other object type` errors are thrown inside `register` method.

That schema also creates `graphql.core.type.schema.GraphqlSchema`. I'm not sure how this is possible, as every time schema is accessed, a new instance is created, but here again looks like 
- thread interference leads to errors: `Schema must contain unique named types but contains multiple types named {}` in `type_map_reducer` function.

Below an example of an error. I don't paste all of them, but that is a good example, as many of them are related to automatically created Connection types, which, I guess, are created independently by separate threads traversing type tree and, so, are not the same types although they do have the same name (which I checked).

issue_title: Cant access meta class only_fields attribute
Consider accepting **kwargs for workers instead of the config dict
issue_body: General idea would be to pop out the config keys used by that worker and assign them to `self.<var>` in `__init__()`. Major benefit I have in mind is that it would provide a clear link between the config settings and the worker, and provide a logical place to document those config settings.

issue_title: Write salt config to a custom "watchmaker" location
issue_body:
Right now we are writing to the default salt config location. This means anyone using salt themselves needs to be aware of what watchmaker does to salt's config and may need to work around the watchmaker configuration in some way.

Salt supports specifying a config directory at runtime, with `-c`. We can write the watchmaker salt config to a custom location and use this parameter when calling salt. This will avoid stepping on anyone's feet, should they be using salt themselves.

I upgraded my django install to version 1.11 and had an issue with AccessLog records not getting created on login.

My app has a signal to catch post_save creation of AccessLog:
`@receiver(post_save, sender=AccessLog, dispatch_uid="AuditEntry creation")
def access_log_creation(sender, instance, created, **kwargs):`

When I ran my tests records were not being created on login.

With django 1.10 I had my "auth" urls setup like this:
`url(r'^accounts/', include('django.contrib.auth.urls')),`

django-axes automatically decorated the login.

My current fix that appears to work is separating the login url into its own entry and decorating it like this:
`url(r'^accounts/login/?$', watch_login(login)),
url(r'^accounts/', include('django.contrib.auth.urls')),`

And `DateTimePicker` widget code:
```python
def add_classes(attrs, classes):
    if 'class' in attrs:
        attrs['class'] += f' {classes}'
    else:
        attrs['class'] = classes


class DateTimePicker(SplitDateTimeWidget):
    datepicker_classes = 'datepicker'
    timepicker_classes = 'timepicker'

    def __init__(self, attrs=None, date_format=None, time_format=None, date_attrs=None, time_attrs=None):

issue_title: How to upload build dependencies too?
issue_body:
I have a package `A` which has other packages (e.g. `B`) as build dependencies. Package `B` is not available as a binary package. When I build `A` with `build_policy` set to `missing` package `B` is build from source as well.

At the end the new package for `A` is uploaded to my repo. Is it possible to automatically upload the new package for `B` as well?

It is somehow not specified on the documentation how the builds are split by pages.

I've tested it and it seems that page N gets the builds with array index i mod N == 0, that is for example for 4 pages:

builder.builds[0] - > Page 1
builder.builds[1] - > Page 2
builder.builds[2] - > Page 3
builder.builds[3] - > Page 4
builder.builds[4] - > Page 1
builder.builds[5] - > Page 2

Please confirm and I can pull request a docs clarification.

Also, is there a way to have any control over this? For example, if I have 2 pages, I might want to split the builds in Page 1 for x86 and Page 2 for x86_64 so they take about the same time, as I also might want to keep variations of Debug or Shared in the same page, to reuse builds that just do different packaging.

Would I have to construct my builds array with x86 on even indexes and x86_64 on odd indexes?

Thanks,
Nuno

issue_title: Julia/TensorBoard contradictory warning about AVX2 from custom compiled version?
issue_body:
- TensorBoard version 1.8.0
 - TensorFlow version 1.8.0 custom compile with bazel, libtensorflow.so
 - OS Platform and version: openSUSE Leap 15
 - Python version 3.6
 
Julia 0.6.3 runs some tests on TensorFlow including loading TensorBoard. When using a plain vanilla  default version of TensorFlow I am warned that my processor can use AVX, AVX2, FMA and SSE, so I compile a custom version of `libtensorflow.so` with bazel. With Julia pointed to the new custom version the warning on load of tensorflow about AVX does not happen, perhaps signalling that it has found a correct binary, however when the TensorBoard test starts it reports that I should compile for AVX2 and FMA.
Examining libtensorflow.so with objdump shows that the .so library contains references to AVX2.
I have reported this in https://github.com/malmaud/TensorFlow.jl/issues/400, but the issue seems to be with TensorBoard and not Julia.
One reason for tensorboard finding a problem is that maybe I have multiple versions of tensorflow available and tensorboard is consulting a different version but I think I only have the one compiled .so. I have not used pip to load a tensorflow into Python. Perhaps I can test further, any suggestions?

Hi,

say I wish to make tensorboard as a service, which means I have a server receive `launching a tensorboard` request, then the server activates a tensorboard server in another thread/process, rather than launching it via `tensorboard` on cmdline as usual.

it is observed that `start_reloading_multiplexer` always has the  `_reload_forever` thread running in background, even if shutdown the tensorboard server. So I simply put a `threading.Event` var over `while` statement instead of `while True`, then the thread is somehow under control. Does it fit into the whole tensorboard scenario? or it is only a specific one for myself.

issue_body:
See Pull request: https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/2082

Observation: often physical devices have more then 1 physical UART

1) The NCP does have support to push the DEBUG LOG upto the host platform over a "reverse channel" 
This is nice but depending upon log volume - the rate of increased traffic causes other problems.

2) In the GRL test harness, it took talks to the CLI and does not expect log messages.
Using a second UART for LOGs will make it easier to debug certain issues.

3) In the POSIX platform, when debugging problems that go wrong in self-test-automation-scripts the LOG feature interferes with and messes up the UNIT test, in the CLI the easiest solution is to provide a CLI command where you can specify a filename to capture the debug log output.

We have this working internal at TI for CC2538 and CC2652 and POSIX platforms and are looking at pushing this up.

Certificate 10.2.13 is used to verify the device is able to properly accept and process energy scan process.

This feature need to be implemented to pass the certificate test.

issue_body:
When `ConnectTimeoutError` is attempted in "napalm/napalm/junos/junos.py" line 109, it references an attribute called `message` which has been deprecated. 

In python2.7:
```

issue_title: TFlearn sequence generator seq seed issue
issue_body:
I've already tried asking on Stack Overflow but no one responded. This seems to be a function that is rarely used and the documentation aren't helpful.

So I've trained a recurrent neural network to classify sentences into two different categories. Now I wanted to use it to regenerate new ones.

I've commented out all unnecessary code.

    import tflearn
    from tflearn.data_utils import to_categorical, pad_sequences
    import pickle
    
    # from sklearn.cross_validation import train_test_split
    #
    # with open('data.pkl','rb') as f:
    #     data = pickle.load(f)
    # with open('label.pkl','rb') as g:
    #     label = pickle.load(g)
    with open('dictionary.pkl','rb') as h:
        dictionary = pickle.load(h)
    #trainX,testX,trainY,testY = train_test_split(data,label,test_size=0.2,
    #                                             random_state=True)
    dicto={}
    for i in range(len(dictionary)):
        dicto[dictionary[i]]=i
    #
    # trainX = pad_sequences(trainX, maxlen=42, value=0.)
    # testX = pad_sequences(testX, maxlen=42, value=0.)
    # trainY = to_categorical(trainY)

    net = tflearn.input_data([None,42])
    net = tflearn.embedding(net, input_dim=2094, output_dim=150)
    net = tflearn.lstm(net,150)
    net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 2, activation='softmax')
    net = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer='adam', learning_rate=0.001,

issue_title: Newline character affects predictions (STDIN and FILE)
issue_body: Hello, Today I found that the version 0.1.0 (the version downloaded in December) do not give the same prediction probabilities whether a line ends with newline (\n). In fact, the probabilities are affected because of the \n. Moreover, this problem affects the label prediction of unseen words during the training. To understand better, here is an example using as input creat89 and edge hill. It should be noted that the word creat89 doesn't exist in the training model, thus it's word vector is full of 0. First using the STDIN method: creat89 -> __label__A   1        __label__B   1.95313e-08 edge hill -> __label__A   0.585938    __label__B   0.412109 If we use a file where the input is (note there is no newline at the end of the file): creat89 edge hill We get the following output from FastTex: __label__A   1        __label__B   1.95313e-08 __label__A   1.95313e-08    __label__B   0.998047 As we ca see, the probability for edge hill changed because there is no new line at the end. So what happens when we have a file (again, no new line at the end of the file): creat89 edge hill creat89 We get the following output from FastTex: __label__A   1        __label__B   1.95313e-08 __label__A   0.585938    __label__B   0.412109 [JUST A NEW LINE PRINTED] We can observe that edge hill gets again the same probability as in the STDIN. However, creat89 in the first case has the probabilities from the STDIN, but in the second case, FastText did not printed anything, the reason, the lack of \n. In fact, if the file or the STDIN is only a newline character (\n), we get the following prediction: __label__A   1        __label__B   1.95313e-08 There is certainly a problem that consists in using \n as a character that belongs to the string, while it is not part of it.

issue_title: Keyword arguments mismatch with C++ function arguments
issue_body:
I have a C++ function with the following signature:
```C++
spv_float well_path_ident(
	ulong nx, ulong ny, spv_float coord, spv_float zcorn,
	spv_float well_info, bool include_well_nodes, const char* strat_traits = "online_tops",
	const ulong min_split_threshold = 0, spv_ulong hit_idx = nullptr
);
```
Here `ulong` is `std::size_t`, and `spv_float, spv_ulong` are `std::shared_ptr` pointers to my custom array type which is inherited from `pybind11::array_t`. I've written custom converters for it that are very similar to `Eigen` binding code. Converters are working as expected in my tests.

Next, I export the function above with the following snippet:
```C++
m.def("well_path_ident", &well_path_ident,
	"nx"_a, "ny"_a, "coord"_a, "zcorn"_a, "well_info"_a, "include_well_nodes"_a = true,
	"mesh_traits"_a = "online_tops", "min_split_threshold"_a = 0, "hit_idx"_a = nullptr
);
```
Everything compiles just fine! `pybind11` correctly counts arguments of `well_path_ident` -- if I make a mistake (for ex. forget one argument) it will notify me with an error at compile time.

But after module is loaded in Python here's what I get with `help(my_module)` command:
```
well_path_ident(...)
        well_path_ident(*args, **kwargs)
        Overloaded function.

        1. well_path_ident(nx: int, ny: int, numpy.ndarray[float64], numpy.ndarray[float64], numpy.ndarray[float64], coord: bool, zcorn: unicode, well_info: int, numpy.ndarray[uint64]) -> numpy.ndarray[float64]
```
(I have one more overload of this function, but it has the same issue).

As you can see, keyword arguments "skip" `numpy.ndarray[float64]` (spv_float) for some reason, and starting from "coord" they bind to wrong C++ function arguments.

I feel that this is related in some way to custom type conversion code, but what really can cause such issue?
Btw, when I try to call this function from Python, I get an exception:
```
RuntimeError: Unknown internal error occurred
```
And actual C++ function isn't called, I can't get into it in debugger.

vdirsyncer, version 0.11.0 (installed via `pip install --user vdirsyncer`)
Python 3.5.1
Arch Linux
Google Calendar

config (sanitized):

[general]

```

vdirsyncer *used* to work, but it has since stopped because it can't find properties for an event. I guess perhaps Google says the event exists, even though it can't be found anywhere?

```
warning: Skipping '/caldav/v2/me@gmail.com/events/040000008200E00074C5B7101A82E008000000001077418DC88CD00100000000000
00000100000009BA262E5883C8A4D92C776A69D335B54.ics', properties are missing.

issue_title: Features to simplify things for chart developers
issue_body:
## Background
I would like the developer experience for Z2JH to be as great as possible. Currently I think chartpress clutters things too much in the local git clone of the repo in Chart.yaml and values.yaml. It is generally quite cumbersome to make a change in the Z2JH repo and use the new chart, and I'd like to improve it. With the following suggested changes to chartpress, we could make a very smooth experience for new developers I think.

## Suggestions
- [ ] **Allow chartpress to be run from a different folder**
  I think we should let chartpress mirror something like `docker build .` allowing a path to be specified, but still default to the CWD. Think of for example `docker build .`, they allow the same thing but specifying `.` is the most common thing to do.
- [ ] **Avoid modifying `values.yaml` in place**
  I think passing the chartpress argument `--images-config images-config.yaml` could be a good way to ask chartpress to output the image name and tag to an external `.yaml` file instead of overwriting the values in place in `values.yaml`.
- [ ] **Avoid modifying `Chart.yaml` in place**
  Is it important that we update the version all the time? Perhaps we can do it only when we are pushing the chart? We could perhaps also restore it after the chart has been pushed? I recall the chart is copied to a folder, perhaps we could only make the `Chart.yaml` in that copied folder be updated, and let the `Chart.yaml` within the original repo remain as it is?
- [ ] **Allow override of ...**
  I was about to write that we should allow override of the repo.git field, but perhaps it is even better to simply allow for an additional chartpress.yaml file that can override fields in the chartpress.yaml within the repo?

issue_title: Please pin dependency versions, not specific dependencies
issue_body:
Hi there! Hundreds of data scientists at Spotify use this package every day. It's really awesome. We make it available internally through a Docker image. 

The problem, similar to https://github.com/googledatalab/pydatalab/issues/386, is that this package's practice of pinning specific dependency versions - rather than version ranges, like every other open source python library from Google - makes it a huge, chronic pain to maintain installations of this library. Here is why:

In addition to pydatalab, we install a few dozen standard pydata packages to our internal docker image for data scientists - pandas, numpy, pandas-gbq, etc. Nearly every time we want to release the image, the pydatalab package's hard dependencies cause an insolvable dependency conflict with some other package. As a result, we have to maintain our own patched version of pydatalab in our internal github and release it to our internal pypi, which is a time consuming pain.

The [official python packaging docs](https://packaging.python.org/discussions/install-requires-vs-requirements/) discourage pinning versions in `install_requires` (unlike `requirements.txt`), and no other Google python package seems to do this:

```
datalab==1.1.2            requires configparser==3.5.0, future==0.16.0, futures==3.0.5, google-api-python-client==1.6.2, google-cloud==0.30.0, google_auth_httplib2==0.0.2, httplib2==0.10.3, ipykernel==4.5.2, jsonschema==2.6.0, mock==2.0.0, oauth2client==2.2.0, pandas-profiling>=1.0.0a2, pandas==0.22.0, plotly==1.12.5, psutil==4.3.0, python-dateutil==2.5.0, pytz>=2015.4, pyyaml==3.11, requests==2.9.1, scikit-image==0.13.0, scikit-learn==0.18.2, seaborn==0.7.0, six==1.10.0, urllib3==1.22
 
  google-api-core==1.1.0    requires google-auth<2.0.0dev,>=0.4.0, googleapis-common-protos<2.0dev,>=1.5.3, protobuf>=3.0.0, pytz, requests<3.0.0dev,>=2.18.0, setuptools>=34.0.0, six>=1.10.0
  
  tensorflow==1.7.0         requires absl-py>=0.1.6, astor>=0.6.0, gast>=0.2.0, grpcio>=1.8.6, numpy>=1.13.3, protobuf>=3.4.0, six>=1.10.0, tensorboard<1.8.0,>=1.7.0, termcolor>=1.1.0, wheel>=0.26
```

In fact, because of the pinning practice, pydatalab 1.1.2's _own_ dependencies appear to be conflicted:

datalab -> requests==2.9.1
datalab -> google-cloud==0.30 -> google-api-core<0.2.0dev,>=0.1.1 -> currently resolves to google-api-core==1.1.0 -> requests<3.0,>=2.18.0

One of our internal image releases is blocked until we either patch and release pydatalab again, or this issue is resolved upstream if you start declaring dependency ranges instead of hard versions (our overwhelming preference, as this reduces the maintenance burden for both of us).

issue_title: JS Client: How to use Raiden from JS?
issue_body: Requirements: Participate in the network w/o being a full Raiden node. Simple JS integration. Working with semi trusted relays is probably an option.
Some problems were seen here: https://github.com/DataMedSci/mcpartools/pull/57

it looks that spot size saved in PLD file is not exactly the same as the sigma of beam position at virtual source. 

PLD file description says that `Spot1` or `SpotID` item (second item in Layer line in PLD file) is just a tag used to differentiate spot sizes (for the same beam energy there may be different machine settings). It looks that it cannot be interpreted as the beam sigma in milimeters as it is done currently in pld2sobp script and in MC generators:

https://github.com/DataMedSci/pymchelper/blob/v0.6.10/pymchelper/utils/pld2sobp.py#L105
```                self.layers.append(Layer(token[1].strip(),  # Spot1 ```

```
class Layer(object):
    def __init__(self, spotsize, energy, meterset, elsum, repaints, elements):
        self.spotsize = float(spotsize)
```

Looking at several PLD files available to me I see that spottag is always set to 3 (for wide range of energies). Assuming 2 meters of air between virtual source position and isocenter, point-like beam will get size (in terms of sigma), following Lynch-Dahl formula:
 - 2.3mm @ 220 MeV
 - 7.2mm @ 60 MeV

I will also cross-checked measured spot sizes at different energy levels, but I don't expect them to be equal to 3mm. It would be necessary to cross-check simulated spot-sizes with what is measured.

issue_title: Dockerized SoS workflow conflict with R system variables when `$HOME` has to be mounted
issue_body:
Okay the story is that my boss just sent me an upsetting message running one of my sos-based docker image. It complains that some R packages are not found -- which my docker image certainly has anyways. Apparently, it was caused by his own `~/.R/Renviron` setting in conflict with my docker's setting. 

The reason I had to mount `$HOME:$HOME` is because SoS has to access `~/.sos` . But you can imaging having to mount `$HOME` to docker just for `~/.sos` will cause many issues. Is there a way to let it point to elsewhere? The cleanest is perhaps to use some bash `env`, if you do not want to add an option? eg.

```
SOS_GLOBAL_DIR=`pwd`/.sos
```

to set it to `./sos` for example. Default to `~/.sos` of course. That way, I can build this `ENV` to my docker file.

for_each={'ID,NID': zip(IDs, NIDs)}
```
returns an error. We should support iterator like `zip` in `for_each`.

issue_title: Read retries cause multiple DELETE requests
issue_body:
### Actual behavior

The default values for the retry / timeout configuration specify `read_timeout=300` (sec) and `read_retries=3`.

This configuration causes the "requests" module to resend DELETE requests if the processing takes longer than the `read_timeout`.

This happens for example for the `Partition.delete()` method for a partition with many NICs that had been started and stopped.

### Expected behavior

A DELETE request should not automatically be re-requested after it is already being processed.

A short term mitigation is to change the default configuration to `read_retries=0`, `read_timeout={some large value}`.

A better solution needs to be discussed.

### Execution environment

* zhmcclient version: 0.12.0.dev3
* Operating system (type+version): RHEL 7.2
* HMC version: 2.13.1
* CPC version: 2.13.1

issue_body:
The vast majority of command groups send the help message when no subcommands are given - they do this with a statement like:
```py
if ctx.invoked_subcommand is None:
    await ctx.send_help()
```

This is really common and honestly, it's unnecessary boilerplate. It gets worse when we have sub-groups, with people often using `isinstance` checks to send help there. 

@mikeshardmind came up with the suggestion to add this to the `group()` decorator instead, but optionally disable it with a flag in case automatically sending help on no invoked subcommand wasn't desired.

We realised this would introduce a somewhat breaking change - all of a sudden, 3rd party cogs who hadn't removed the call to `ctx.send_help()` would be getting duplicate help being sent. For this reason, we've proposed a grace period where the flag will default to `False`, and will be set to `True` for beta 17.

issue_title: Print out whether there are sample ids
Add example for using batches if you want to accessing all the genome
issue_body:
I'm using the library to iterate over the entire genome. It's *way* faster to do this in batches, rather than calling `.compute()` on each variant in turn. I.e. I now do

`bgen['genotype'][x:(x+batch_size),:,0:2].compute()`

rather than this slow iteration

```
for i in range(x, x+batch_size):
    bgen['genotype'][i,:,0:2].compute()
```

In fact, the `.compute()` call seems takes almost the same time regardless of whether it is getting 1 or 100 variants. It's obvious in hindsight, but it's probably worth saying that in the documentation, or even giving an example. Of course, if the batch size is too high, you run out of memory, so you would have to recommend tweaking this for the machine you are running on. But I had at least a 100x speedup in my code by batching the `.compute()` requests in this way.

issue_title: SQL errors should not cause Internal Server Error
issue_body:
Hi all,

I have the models and relationships as shown below. As you can see, the `parent_id` column is a non-nullable ForeignKey. When I attempt to delete a parent for one of these children, the response is a `500 Internal Server Error` with HTML that includes the actual SQL error.

For proper API behavior, an underlying SQL error should produce a `4xx` error that returns the SQL error the same way that a validation error would be produced by Eve, i.e. with the `_error` property.

```python
class CommonColumns(Base):
    __abstract__ = True
    _created = Column(DateTime, default=func.now())
    _updated = Column(DateTime, default=func.now(), onupdate=func.now())
    _etag = Column(String(40))


class Child(CommonColumns):
    __tablename__ = 'children'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    title = Column(String(128))
    parent_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('parents.id'), nullable=False)
    parent = relationship('Parents', back_populates='children')


class Parents(CommonColumns):
    __tablename__ = 'parents'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    title = Column(String(128))
    children = relationship('Child', back_populates='parent')
```

To implement this, I believe the `sqlalchemy.exc.IntegrityError` should be caught by Eve-SQLAlchemy and wrapped up in an Eve error response format.

    - What Not

issue_title: ProbabilitySampler does not work with nested spans
issue_body:
## Description
When `ProbabilitySampler` decides not to sample, the following fails with `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'span'`:

```python
with tracer.span(name='span1') as span1:
    do_something_to_trace()
    with span1.span(name='span1_child1') as span1_child1:
        do_something_to_trace()
```

Of course, since if the sampler decides not to sample, then the tracer will be replaced with `NoopTracer` here: https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/cfcfda584ba984e6f0e89684120c1ea94271b2c9/opencensus/trace/tracer.py#L88 . As a consequence, spans are managed by `NullContextManager`s: https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/ad75bf58fc297eeb2979e3973c09907772cb7acc/opencensus/trace/tracers/noop_tracer.py#L48. When that `ContextManager` is entered, nothing is bound to the `with` expression because `__enter__` does not return (returns `None`) here: https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/cfcfda584ba984e6f0e89684120c1ea94271b2c9/opencensus/trace/tracers/base.py#L74-L76  

So this is why in the example `span1` is `None` if the sampler decides not to sample. 

## Environment
Python version: 3.5.1 (on CentOS 7 based Docker image)

pip freeze:
```
cachetools==2.0.1
certifi==2018.1.18
chardet==3.0.4
dill==0.2.7.1
future==0.16.0
google-api-core==0.1.4
google-auth==1.4.1
google-cloud-core==0.28.1
google-cloud-trace==0.17.0
google-gax==0.15.16
googleapis-common-protos==1.5.3
grpcio==1.11.0
grpcio-tools==1.11.0
idna==2.6
opencensus==0.1.4
ply==3.8
protobuf==3.5.2.post1
pyasn1==0.4.2
pyasn1-modules==0.2.1
pytz==2018.4
requests==2.18.4
rsa==3.4.2
six==1.11.0
urllib3==1.22
```

## Proposal
I am really not that familiar with the code, but I would probably let the exporters know about the sampling decision in `SpanData`: https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/span_data.py#L19 and move any logic that depends on the sampling decision into the base exporter. Not sure if this is the best approach though. Another way would be just to let `NullContextManager` bind to `Span` objects that implement the same interface, but they may have a more specific type that indicates they are not to be reported -- then the type check could be done in https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/tracers/context_tracer.py#L99 . It's not trivial, but surely solvable. 

Please do let me know if I missed anything or if you need help fixing this.

issue_title: CasADi backend: Delay operator only supports states, not indexed states or expressions
issue_body:
This fails, for example:

```
delayed = delay(array[1], 3600);
```

issue_title: Quickest way to play against the best bots?
issue_body:
I'm here after checking out the trailer for the upcoming bot tournament.

I gave RLbot a shot last year and back then I remember there weren't a lot of human level bots, now I'm seeing aerials in the trailers.

First of all, fantastic work! Second of all, how do I play against some of these amazing bots?

The following doesn't seem to hold

> When using a lexer (standard or contextual), it is the grammar-author's responsibility to make sure the literals don't collide, or that if they do, they are matched in the desired order. Literals are matched in an order according to the following criteria:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import lark

grammar = """
A.2: "a"
WORD: ("a".."z")+

start: (A | WORD)+
"""

earley = lark.Lark(grammar, parser='earley')
lalr = lark.Lark(grammar, parser='lalr')

text = 'abc'

earley_tree = earley.parse(text)
print('Earley:', earley_tree)

lalr_tree = lalr.parse(text)
print('LALR', lalr_tree)

print(earley_tree == lalr_tree)
```
```
Earley: Tree(start, [Token(WORD, 'a'), Token(WORD, 'b'), Token(WORD, 'c')])
LALR Tree(start, [Token(WORD, 'abc')])
False
```

I'd expect the following:
> LALR Tree(start, [Token(A, 'a'), Token(WORD, 'bc')])

The 'A' token seems to go missing here because it's considered to be embedded in 'WORD':
https://github.com/erezsh/lark/blob/255ef0d973a140b5be07cb4d97c5bf8f8e20788e/lark/lexer.py#L153

(Unrelated: I expected the same result for both parsers, not sure if the Earley tokenization is another bug or simply the way an Earley parser is supposed to work)

issue_title: error in ｗｅｒｏｂｏｔ when send voice message
issue_body: when send a voice type message, it always come out a error that as "40005 invalid file type hint."

Logging is still a bit of a mess. The "colored logs" module installs a logger that is global, I believe, so that it persists between different execution context creations. This can cause a lot of confusion (for me at least).

It would be fantastic to have a new general model for dealing with python's BS, global-state-polluting, java-naming-conventioned, logging API
